EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure in the Mississippi Valley tracked northeast through the Ohio Valley. A strong surface low developed in eastern Kentucky. Mixed freezing rain and sleet early on on the evening of the 19th quickly transitioned to a heavy snow overnight. This system was followed by bitter cold and sub-zero wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer southeast of Tollesboro measured an inch of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure in the Mississippi Valley tracked northeast through the Ohio Valley. A strong surface low developed in eastern Kentucky. Mixed freezing rain and sleet early on on the evening of the 19th quickly transitioned to a heavy snow overnight. This system was followed by bitter cold and sub-zero wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CVG ASOS measured 6.5 inches of snow, along with freezing rain and sleet. A social media post north of Walton had 5.3 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure in the Mississippi Valley tracked northeast through the Ohio Valley. A strong surface low developed in eastern Kentucky. Mixed freezing rain and sleet early on on the evening of the 19th quickly transitioned to a heavy snow overnight. This system was followed by bitter cold and sub-zero wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on surrounding observations, it is estimated that the northern and western portions of the county received over 4 inches of snow, along with a glaze of ice from freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure in the Mississippi Valley tracked northeast through the Ohio Valley. A strong surface low developed in eastern Kentucky. Mixed freezing rain and sleet early on on the evening of the 19th quickly transitioned to a heavy snow overnight. This system was followed by bitter cold and sub-zero wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant snow and ice created hazardous conditions across the county. A social media post from Alexandria recorded 2 inches of snow before the freezing rain and rain melted much of it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure in the Mississippi Valley tracked northeast through the Ohio Valley. A strong surface low developed in eastern Kentucky. Mixed freezing rain and sleet early on on the evening of the 19th quickly transitioned to a heavy snow overnight. This system was followed by bitter cold and sub-zero wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter near Crittenden measured 3 inches of snow before freezing rain and rain cut back recorded amounts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass was pushed into the Ohio Valley behind a cold front. Temperatures within a few degrees of zero combined with wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph, creating wind chills from 20 to 30 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cincinnati airport ASOS CVG reported a wind chill of 27 below zero. On 2/2/19, two elderly brothers were found frozen to death in Petersburg. The official date of death is listed as the day they were found, but the most likely date of death would have been 1/30, possibly late on the 29th as there were two receipts dated the 29th that were found with them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass was pushed into the Ohio Valley behind a cold front. Temperatures within a few degrees of zero combined with wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph, creating wind chills from 20 to 30 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills north of Park Hills dropped to 31 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and isolated thunderstorms moved into eastern Kentucky late this afternoon. One of these storms became strong enough to produce sub-severe hail in Mount Vernon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A citizen reported nickel sized hail in Mount Vernon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low pressure system moved from the lower Ohio River Valley to the Great Lakes on the 14th, pushing a cold front through late that evening. Ahead of the cold front, strong to severe thunderstorms developed, producing mainly scattered wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along Straight Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of frost occurred during the early morning hours as temperatures dipped into the lower 30's. Agricultural experts reported no significant effects on protected crops or  fruit trees. The only reported damage was some minor cold injury that affected random asparagus spears, but there was nothing widespread.  A ridge of high pressure extending from the Gulf coast northward to the lower Ohio and mid Mississippi Valleys provided clear skies and light winds. These conditions were conducive for frost.

Multiple rounds of localized heavy rain occurred over portions of Bath County through the overnight hours, resulting in flash flooding. One rain gauge near Owingsville measured 7.23 inches of rain between 2:30 a.m. and 4:30 a.m. This led to water entering a home as well as washing out a bridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county Emergency Manager reported water from a nearby creek rushing inside of a home in Pebble. This occurred as a rain gauge on East Fork Road, southwest of Pebble, recorded 7.23 inches of rainfall in a two hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms slid from northwest to southeast across eastern Kentucky late this evening into the overnight hours, resulting in an isolated occurrence of flash flooding near Flat Creek in Bath County due to training.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local Emergency Management observed nearly a foot of swiftly flowing water over Flat Creek Road north of U.S. Highway 60.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hard freeze was observed across most of western Kentucky. Low temperatures were in the middle to upper 20's in most areas. Agricultural experts reported some damage to peach buds, but not enough to ruin the peach crop. More than one peach grower reported highly variable peach bud survival, ranging from some trees with no bud survival to other trees with no apparent cold injury. Early crops such as cucumbers were generally unaffected if preventive measures were taken against freeze damage. High pressure from the Ozark Mountains through the Ohio Valley created ideal conditions for frost and freezing temperatures. Winds were light with clear skies.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains toward the end of December sent the Ohio and Green Rivers above flood stage from December into the first part of January. The flooding was minor, consisting mostly of flooded bottomland fields, woodlands, and public park and recreation areas. Some lesser-traveled roads in the bottomlands were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked south of the region through the Tennessee Valley on the evening of Saturday, January 12th, followed by a secondary surface low overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CVG airport measured 6.9 inches of snow. A period of rain and freezing rain significantly cut down on snow amounts but caused additional complications of their own.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked south of the region through the Tennessee Valley on the evening of Saturday, January 12th, followed by a secondary surface low overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Maysville, two inches of snow was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front south of the Ohio River became a focus for deeper moisture over the area, which got wrung out as light accumulations of snow when an upper level shortwave crossed the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter south of Union measured 2 inches of snow. The CoCoRaHS observer northwest of Walton measured 1.5 inches of snow, while another east of Union measured 0.9 inches. A ham radio operator in Union reported 1.2 inches, while the observer augmenting the KCVG ASOS measured 0.6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance crossed the Ohio Valley on Sunday, March 3rd. This provided enough energy to wring out a few inches of snow across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer augmenting the KCVG ASOS reported that 2 inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved east through the region ahead of an advancing cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and a fence were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved east through the region ahead of an advancing cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof was lifted off a garage along Woodlyn Hills Drive in Erlanger. A pine tree was also uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved east through the region ahead of an advancing cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were uprooted along the AA Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed this afternoon as an upper level trough moved across eastern Kentucky. Repeated rounds of thunderstorms and heavy rain led to isolated flash flooding in southeastern Montgomery County, while a portion of Pike County experienced flooding issues. Elsewhere, isolated small hail up to nickel size occurred due to cold temperatures aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch relayed a report of penny sized hail south of Booneville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a strong low pressure system that was approaching the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of heavy rain moved into Bell County on the evening of May 11th, following earlier rainfall. This led to a couple of mudslides, as well as minor flooding near poorly draining culverts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported water backing up onto the roadway at the intersection of Kentucky Highways 1491 and 190 near Clear Creek Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of strong thunderstorms developed across south central and south eastern Kentucky late this afternoon and early evening. One of these storms briefly became severe near Somerset, producing damage to a couple of trees and trampolines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county Emergency Manager reported a couple of damaged trees just southeast of Somerset, one uprooted and another broken midway up its trunk. A couple of trampolines were also blown around on Regal Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure center tracked from eastern Arkansas across middle Tennessee during the day, then into southeast Kentucky on the evening of the 19th. As colder air quickly pushed southward, rain changed to snow during the late afternoon and early evening hours. Travel impacts were high due to the combination of snow, strong wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph, and rapidly falling temperatures. Roads  became snow and ice covered, and visibility was frequently around one mile for a few hours. Generally from 1 to 3 inches fell across western Kentucky. Peak wind gusts were measured up to 46 mph at the Paducah airport and around 40 mph at most other sites. Temperatures fell into the upper 20's by the end of the snowstorm, producing wind chills in the teens.

A line of thunderstorms developed early in the morning from western Arkansas into south central Missouri ahead of a strong cold front. This line of storms intensified as it moved east-northeast across the mid Mississippi Valley later in the morning. Some severe weather continued through the midday hours as the line progressed up the lower Ohio Valley. This line of storms was the final round of heavy rain, which capped a very wet couple days that resulted in storm totals of 2 to 4 inches. Isolated higher amounts up to near 5 inches were reported. Isolated flooding was reported in some areas. Although instability was weak, very strong winds aloft facilitated isolated severe storms and a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust to 60 mph was measured at the National Weather Service office in Paducah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding continued from late January into the first week of February on parts of the Ohio River. The flooding was minor, consisting mostly of flooded bottomland fields, woodlands, and public park and recreation areas. Some lesser-traveled roads in the bottomlands were flooded. Heavy rain impacted the region shortly after the middle part of January, causing this particular flood wave.

Widespread snow, mixed at times with sleet, spread across western Kentucky during the late afternoon and early evening hours on January 11th. This was in response to surface low pressure that developed across the Texas and Oklahoma region. This low then moved east-northeastward across the Missouri Bootheel by early evening on the 12th. Warmer temperatures at the surface and aloft gradually changed the precipitation to rain during the overnight hours of the 11th into the pre-dawn hours of the 12th. Sleet was not widespread enough to significantly lower snowfall amounts. The heaviest snowfall amounts from this system were in the Henderson and Owensboro area, where two to three inches fell. Amounts were 1 to 2 inches in the Paducah to Madisonville corridor, and less than an inch further south. Along and north of the Paducah to Madisonville corridor, there were significant travel impacts during the evening hours and into the overnight hours. Snow-covered roads led to some accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved slowly northward from the Gulf Coast states on the night of the 19th, reaching the lower Ohio Valley on the morning of the 20th. This warm front brought the third widespread heavy rainfall event so far in February. Rainfall totals over a 24-hour period were from 2 to 3 inches in much of western Kentucky, with locally up to 3.5 inches west of Kentucky Lake. This rainfall fell on moist ground, causing many creeks and smaller rivers to flood. The flooding was exacerbated by very high levels on the Ohio and Mississippi Rivers, which caused tributary rivers and creeks to back up into western Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were flooded around the county, mainly in flood-prone areas and near creeks. Water was over Highway 80 just west of U.S. 51 in Arlington.

Along and north of the Paducah to Madisonville corridor, there were significant travel impacts during the evening hours and into the overnight hours. Snow-covered roads led to some accidents.

Freezing drizzle continued to linger behind a cold front through the 14th and into the 15th. This led to patchy icy spots on roads across portions of eastern Kentucky, most notably Letcher County. Kentucky Highway 15 was closed in Isom for a period on the morning of the 15th as an ambulance hit ice on a bridge and crashed. Four injuries were reported. Additionally, U.S. Highway 119 on top of Whitesburg/Pine Mountain was impassable as the roadway was a solid sheet of ice.

As a swath of precipitation moved into eastern Kentucky this morning, temperatures remained below freezing in the higher elevations of Harlan County, mainly above 2,500 feet. This continued into early afternoon before temperatures warmed above freezing and transitioned all precipitation to rain.||U.S. Highways 421 and 119 on Pine Mountain became snow covered from late morning into early afternoon, prior to pavement temperatures warming above freezing. This period of slick roads led to numerous vehicles becoming stuck while causing a loaded coal truck to jackknife. A crash occurred on U.S. Highway 119 close to the Bell County line.

A deepening low pressure center tracked from eastern Arkansas across middle Tennessee during the day, then into southeast Kentucky on the evening of the 19th. As colder air quickly pushed southward, rain changed to snow during the late afternoon and early evening hours. Travel impacts were high due to the combination of snow, strong wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph, and rapidly falling temperatures. Roads  became snow and ice covered, and visibility was frequently around one mile for a few hours. Generally from 1 to 3 inches fell across western Kentucky. Peak wind gusts were measured up to 46 mph at the Paducah airport and around 40 mph at most other sites. Temperatures fell into the upper 20's by the end of the snowstorm, producing wind chills in the teens.

Persistent rain fell across much of eastern Kentucky this day, with amounts totaling 1 to 1.5 inches. This led to minor flooding across portions of Magoffin and Pulaski Counties due to the antecedent wet conditions. Additionally, Kentucky Highway 615 in Powell County was closed due to a mudslide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported standing water over the road at the entrance of Jellicoe Branch Road on Kentucky Highway 1081 near Flat Fork.

Severe storms developed across eastern Kentucky during the evening hours of June 5, 2019. These thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching frontal boundary.  The main threat early on was large hail.  An organized line of showers and storms then moved in from the north, producing more localized wind damage.||Significant hail up to 2 inches in diameter was reported in Wolfe County with the early storms.  There were also numerous trees blown down in areas, generally along and north of the Mountain Parkway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported trees down from Rogers to southwest of Campton, including one on a cabin near Rogers and one on Sandy Ridge Road southwest of Campton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a brief break in the rain on February 9, light rain began to once again impact eastern Kentucky on February 10. This continued through the 12th, causing additional mudslides across portions of Perry, Floyd, and Magoffin Counties. The largest of these was south of Salyersville on Kentucky Highway 7 in Magoffin County, where an entire hill gave way and caused the closure of the road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Department of Highways reported a mudslide on Kentucky Highway 80 east of Hazard, resulting in the closure of the road for over one day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat indices peaked between 105 and 112 degrees across most of western Kentucky except the Owensboro and Henderson area during the afternoon hours. The highest heat indices were over the Mississippi River counties, where the peak heat index ranged from 110 to 112 degrees. Actual air temperatures reached the lower to mid 90's. Dew points were in the mid 70's to near 80, which resulted in the very high heat indices. At mid levels of the atmosphere, a large ridge of high pressure dominated the southern half of the United States. A weak surface warm front over the lower Ohio Valley caused dew points to pool into the mid 70's to near 80.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a couple day dry spell, rainfall amounts of up to 1-1.5 inches once again fell across portions of southeast Kentucky. This led to renewed flooding issues and additional mud/rock slides in Rockcastle, McCreary, Perry, and Harlan Counties.||This round of rainfall prompted the Perry County Judge-Executive to declare a local state of emergency for the county as damages from this and previous events totaled over $100,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported water over Mt. Zion Church Road outside of Renfro Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow and some mixed precipitation overspread portions of the Ohio Valley on the morning of the 10th. This occurred ahead of a warm front which changed this snow to rain south of the I-70 corridor by afternoon. Snow continued into the evening along the I-70 corridor with generally 2 to 3 inches of accumulation. Significantly less snow occurred further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cincinnati airport KCVG measured 0.6 inches of snow before it changed to rain.

The highest heat indices were over the Mississippi River counties, where the peak heat index ranged from 110 to 112 degrees. Actual air temperatures reached the lower to mid 90's. Dew points were in the mid 70's to near 80, which resulted in the very high heat indices. At mid levels of the atmosphere, a large ridge of high pressure dominated the southern half of the United States. A weak surface warm front over the lower Ohio Valley caused dew points to pool into the mid 70's to near 80.

Following a three day period of well below freezing temperatures, warmer air and precipitation spread north across eastern Kentucky. Cold air remained locked in place across portions of the Interstate 64 corridor, resulting in a period of light snow and freezing rain from morning through mid afternoon. This resulted in icy roads and multiple vehicle accidents, most notably between mile markers 120 to 136 on Interstate 64 in Bath and Rowan Counties.
Several waves of low pressure moved along a stalled front, bringing a prolonged period of heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of Oneonta Road and Truesdell Road was closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of showers, including embedded thunderstorms, worked across eastern Kentucky from February 6th into the 7th along a slow-moving frontal boundary. A thunderstorm briefly became severe near and north of Somerset, producing tree damage. Gusty winds continued throughout the evening of the 7th, which when combined with saturated soils, led to a tree falling onto a mobile home in Laurel County. Rainfall amounts near two inches fell across portions of southeast Kentucky, leading to standing water and a few rock/mud slides. These produced road closures in Harlan, Knott, and Perry Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local Kentucky Transportation Cabinet officials reported an active landslide along Kentucky Highway 7 in Topmost. This forced the road down to one lane for five days. This was likely caused by 1 to 1.5 inches of rain on top of saturated terrain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms developed across eastern Kentucky during the evening hours of June 5, 2019. These thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching frontal boundary.  The main threat early on was large hail.  An organized line of showers and storms then moved in from the north, producing more localized wind damage.||Significant hail up to 2 inches in diameter was reported in Wolfe County with the early storms.  There were also numerous trees blown down in areas, generally along and north of the Mountain Parkway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A citizen received 2 inches of rain in about half an hour, leading to several small mudslides southwest of Jackson. This includes one on Kentucky Highway 2469.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds developed from Lake Cumberland into the Bluegrass region, as well as farther east to near Salyersville this afternoon and early evening. Damage included several downed trees, one of these falling onto a house near Somerset. An out building was also destroyed in Fleming County. Multiple rounds of heavy rain led to an instance of flash flooding near Somerset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported that Patterson Branch Road was inundated with flowing water southwest of Somerset.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Throughout the evening of the 13th and all day on the 14th, strong south gradient winds associated with the strong low pressure system gusted up to 50 mph at times. The low pressure center tracked northeast across the Plains to the Great Lakes region. Measured peak wind gusts at airport sites included 54 mph at Madisonville, 52 mph at Central City, 51 mph at Owensboro, 48 mph at Murray, 47 mph at Hopkinsville and Marion, and 46 mph at Paducah and Mayfield. In Daviess County, a tree fell on a house about four miles west of Philpot. In Muhlenberg County, trees were uprooted in Central City. In Calloway County, several tree branches were down in the Murray area. In Graves County, an old vacant building collapsed in downtown Mayfield. The area around the collapsed building was evacuated while a gas leak was capped off. In Marshall County, the strong winds contributed to a crash involving a semi on Interstate 24 on the night of the 13th. Strong crosswinds caused a semi truck and trailer to roll over, blocking both eastbound lanes near mile marker 21. Two other semis were unable to stop, and they struck the overturned semi. One driver was transported to a local hospital. The eastbound lanes were reopened about nine hours later.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed along Limaburg Road between Conner Road and Conrad Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving line of thunderstorms produced locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 3 mile road underpass was flooded at I-275.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system produced multiple weather hazards. Scattered supercell thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front on the morning and early afternoon of the 14th, producing isolated wind damage, large hail, and several tornadoes. The supercells were preceded by widespread rain and thunderstorms the previous night, which caused some pockets of flooding in western Kentucky. The low pressure center tracked northeast across the Plains to the Great Lakes region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down on Highway 109 in western Union County, where a semi truck and trailer was blown over. The driver sustained minor injuries. Where the tornado crossed Highway 947 a couple miles northwest of Morganfield, a Ford Taurus sedan was picked up and flipped onto its roof. The car was moved approximately 25 feet. Along the path of the tornado, dozens of trees were snapped or uprooted, and numerous power poles were broken. The roof of a farm building was partially ripped off. The tornado was widely seen and videoed by local residents. Peak winds in the tornado were estimated near 110 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Along and northwest of a line from Owensboro to Murray, heat indices peaked between 101 and 107 degrees during the afternoon hours. A combination of temperatures in the mid 90's and dew points in the mid 70's produced these hazardous heat indices. A southwest wind flow of hot and humid air ahead of a cold front was responsible for this first potentially dangerous heat event of the season.

Numerous thunderstorms developed during the mid afternoon hours, with a few of these reaching severe limits and subsequently resulting in wind damage. The worst of this occurred in Bell County near Pineville where large trees were blown onto several vehicles. A few homes and buildings were also damaged. As a result of the damage, the city's Independence Day Festival was cancelled.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported numerous large trees downed in Pineville, resulting in damage to several personal and official vehicles as well as damage to a few homes. A local business lost its roof as a result of the high winds. Due to the damage, city officials cancelled the Fourth of July Festival that was planned for the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms moving into southeastern Kentucky out of the Tennessee Valley brought more heavy rain to much of southeastern Kentucky. This resulted in Corbin experiencing flash flooding on Master Street.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A citizen reported flowing water blocking Master Street in Corbin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms intensified in coverage and intensity during the evening of July 17th, continuing into the early morning hours of July 18th. A couple of rounds of heavy rainfall occurred. One was near Kayjay in Knox County, where water entered residences and caused a road closure as 2.5 inches of rain fell. Bell County also experienced road impacts from swiftly flowing flood waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A broadcast media outlet relayed reports of quickly flowing flood waters inundating Kentucky Highway 225 near Kayjay. Additionally, water entered residences in and around town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across far southeast Kentucky late this afternoon and evening. Slow storm motions led to one organized storm producing copious amounts of rain in Blackey, resulting in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire Department officials observed deep and swift moving flood waters along portions of Kentucky Highway 7 in Blackey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of rounds of showers and thunderstorms fell in and around Elkhorn City and Ashcamp this evening, on top of previously saturated ground. This led to flooding and flash flooding as much of this rainfall instantaneously became runoff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management reported water overflowing creek banks and crossing the roadway while washing out driveways to two homes just northwest of Ashcamp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major river flooding subsided in March. The rainy weather pattern that dominated January and February became less active in March. The winter of 2018-19 ended up being the fourth wettest winter on record at Paducah, where records go back to 1937. February was the second wettest February on record at Paducah. Record rainfall in the Cumberland and Tennessee Valleys sent a large volume of water into Kentucky and Barkley Lakes. Both lakes crested around 367 feet, 8 feet above their summer pool stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding on the Ohio and Green Rivers persisted from February into March. The Ohio River crested at Owensboro during February. On the Green River, the crest did not occur until the first day of March. Low-lying woodlands and fields in the river bottomlands were flooded. In Daviess County, about three dozen roads were closed by high water. The closed roads included Lower River Road from U.S. Route 60 to Griffith Station, Rockport Ferry Road, Crooked Creek Road, and other secondary county roads.

Record rainfall in the Cumberland and Tennessee Valleys sent a large volume of water into Kentucky and Barkley Lakes. Both lakes crested around 367 feet, 8 feet above their summer pool stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued on the Ohio River from February into March. At the Owensboro river gage, the river crested on February 17th. Low-lying woodlands and fields in the river bottomlands were flooded. Numerous roads were flooded, including some state roads. Backwater from the Ohio River caused flooding near the mouth of the Green River, which flooded roads in eastern Henderson County. Flooded roads extended inland to near Hebbardsville.

The area around the collapsed building was evacuated while a gas leak was capped off. In Marshall County, the strong winds contributed to a crash involving a semi on Interstate 24 on the night of the 13th. Strong crosswinds caused a semi truck and trailer to roll over, blocking both eastbound lanes near mile marker 21. Two other semis were unable to stop, and they struck the overturned semi. One driver was transported to a local hospital. The eastbound lanes were reopened about nine hours later.

Thunderstorms increased across western Kentucky during the afternoon hours. An upper level disturbance over Missouri was the primary reason for the increase. Favorably strong winds aloft overcame relatively weak instability to produce isolated damaging winds along a line of storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down around the Beechmont and Drakesboro areas. There was one motor vehicle accident due to the debris. Power lines were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance allowed for precipitation to develop across the Tennessee Valley into eastern Kentucky late on March 7th into March 8th. With cold air in place north of a stationary frontal boundary, this led to the precipitation beginning as snow. Warmer air moved in from the south during the morning of the 8th, allowing the snow to transition to rain by the afternoon.||Portions of Menifee and Rowan Counties received near 4 inches of snow, causing a minor traffic issue at Kentucky Highway 801 at Interstate 64 in Rowan County. One heavier band of snow materialized between Rowan and Menifee Counties, with Big Woods in Menifee County reporting 4 inches of snow. Meanwhile, locations in the higher terrain of far eastern and southeastern Kentucky saw lesser amounts, but impacts were felt in Letcher County on U.S. Highway 23 near Jenkins on Pine Mountain. A tractor-trailer jack-knifed, subsequently leaking fuel, while additional motorists were stranded.

Moderate to major river flooding subsided in March. The rainy weather pattern that dominated January and February became less active in March. The winter of 2018-19 ended up being the fourth wettest winter on record at Paducah, where records go back to 1937. February was the second wettest February on record at Paducah. Record rainfall in the Cumberland and Tennessee Valleys sent a large volume of water into Kentucky and Barkley Lakes. Both lakes crested around 367 feet, 8 feet above their summer pool stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding subsided along the Ohio River after cresting in February. About eight state roads were closed, including Highways 667, 668, 1452, 1637, 871, and 1508. Large areas of agricultural land were flooded, but it was mostly dormant outside of planting season.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major river flooding subsided in March. The rainy weather pattern that dominated January and February became less active in March. The winter of 2018-19 ended up being the fourth wettest winter on record at Paducah, where records go back to 1937. February was the second wettest February on record at Paducah. Record rainfall in the Cumberland and Tennessee Valleys sent a large volume of water into Kentucky and Barkley Lakes. Both lakes crested around 367 feet, 8 feet above their summer pool stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding of the Ohio River diminished after cresting in February. At the Shawneetown, IL gage, the river crested on February 20. The ferry from Hardin County, IL to Crittenden County was shut down due to high water for an extended period of time. Several state roads and numerous county roads were flooded. Backwater flooding along the Tradewater River affected areas near the Webster County line. Large areas of agricultural land were flooded. Crop losses were negligible since planting was not yet underway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center moved northeast from Kansas to northern Illinois. Strong southwest winds occurred in advance of a cold front that trailed south from the low. The strongest winds occurred in the afternoon and evening hours. Peak measured wind gusts included 50 mph at Elkton, 47 mph at Henderson, 46 mph at Mayfield, Fulton, and Calhoun, 44 mph at Paducah, Owensboro, and Central City, and 43 mph at Marion. These measurements were taken at airport automated sites or mesonet sites.

Gusty southwest winds occurred this afternoon ahead of an approaching cold front. These winds, at times gusting up to 40-45 mph, led to isolated damage southwest of Monticello in Wayne County. Fallen trees took down power lines on Kentucky Highway 1009 between Jennings Hollow Road and Kentucky Highway 200, resulting in the closure of the road late this afternoon into the evening.

Moderate to major river flooding subsided in March. The rainy weather pattern that dominated January and February became less active in March. The winter of 2018-19 ended up being the fourth wettest winter on record at Paducah, where records go back to 1937. February was the second wettest February on record at Paducah. Record rainfall in the Cumberland and Tennessee Valleys sent a large volume of water into Kentucky and Barkley Lakes. Both lakes crested around 367 feet, 8 feet above their summer pool stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged major river flood gradually subsided in March after cresting in February. Information on the February crest height and on total property damage estimates is contained in the February report. The flood affected numerous homes and businesses in Livingston County. The primary focus of flood-fighting efforts revolved around the town of Smithland, the county seat. A makeshift levee was built along the river, requiring a multi-week closure of U.S. Highway 60 through town. The governor of Kentucky toured the site of the levee construction, which required the assistance of numerous state and federal agencies. Repeated heavy rainfall in west Kentucky and backwater from the Ohio, Cumberland, and Tennessee Rivers caused widespread flooding in the southern part of Livingston County. Numerous roads were closed around the county, especially in the flood plain. Some of the largest state roads that were closed included U.S. Highway 60 in Smithland and State Route 70. The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers stopped all river traffic on the Ohio River at Smithland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major river flooding subsided in March. The rainy weather pattern that dominated January and February became less active in March. The winter of 2018-19 ended up being the fourth wettest winter on record at Paducah, where records go back to 1937. February was the second wettest February on record at Paducah. Record rainfall in the Cumberland and Tennessee Valleys sent a large volume of water into Kentucky and Barkley Lakes. Both lakes crested around 367 feet, 8 feet above their summer pool stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ohio River remained above flood stage all month after cresting in February. Information on the February flood crest and the total property damage estimate is contained in the February report. Major flooding on the Ohio River closed numerous county roads and a few state roads. U.S. Highway 60/62 was closed from Wickliffe to the Ohio River bridge to Cairo, Illinois. This road serves as a primary link between Kentucky and Illinois. A detour of about 80 miles was required for commuters in the area. Many hunting cabins in the river bottoms were flooded, but only a small number of houses (fewer than 20) were affected. The Ballard Wildlife Management Area was closed.

Along and northwest of a line from Owensboro to Murray, heat indices peaked between 101 and 107 degrees during the afternoon hours. A combination of temperatures in the mid 90's and dew points in the mid 70's produced these hazardous heat indices. A southwest wind flow of hot and humid air ahead of a cold front was responsible for this first potentially dangerous heat event of the season.

Moderate to major river flooding subsided in March. The rainy weather pattern that dominated January and February became less active in March. The winter of 2018-19 ended up being the fourth wettest winter on record at Paducah, where records go back to 1937. February was the second wettest February on record at Paducah. Record rainfall in the Cumberland and Tennessee Valleys sent a large volume of water into Kentucky and Barkley Lakes. Both lakes crested around 367 feet, 8 feet above their summer pool stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued on the Green River from February into March. At the Calhoun river gage, the river crested at 30.69 feet on the 1st. Flood stage there is 23 feet. Several roads were closed in the flood plain. Thousands of acres were flooded in the Island, Rumsey, and Jewel City areas. The acreage was primarily farm fields. Since planting season was not underway when flooding began, there was little if any crop damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding on the Green River gradually subsided after cresting in February. Information on the February crest height is contained in the February report. Some local roads were closed, and considerable flooding of low-lying woods and fields occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major river flooding subsided in March. The rainy weather pattern that dominated January and February became less active in March. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The level of man-made Kentucky Lake was raised to 367 feet, about 8 feet above the normal summer pool stage. Low-lying parks, campgrounds, and marina parking lots near the lake were flooded. Some lake access roads were underwater. The lake level was still several feet below the record set during the spring of 2011 flood.

One driver was transported to a local hospital. The eastbound lanes were reopened about nine hours later.

Strong northwest winds gusted from 40 to 50 mph behind a strong cold front. The following are some of the measured peak wind gusts at mesonet sites and airport sites: 50 mph at Marion, 49 mph near Dixon, Central City, and Calhoun, 47 mph at Fort Campbell and Princeton, 46 mph at Murray, 45 mph at Fulton and Henderson, and 44 mph at Paducah. The strong winds caused some minor damage. A shed was damaged at Fredonia in Caldwell County. Some fascia was stripped off a house near Murray.

A strong low pressure system produced multiple weather hazards. Scattered supercell thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front on the morning and early afternoon of the 14th, producing isolated wind damage, large hail, and several tornadoes. The supercells were preceded by widespread rain and thunderstorms the previous night, which caused some pockets of flooding in western Kentucky. The low pressure center tracked northeast across the Plains to the Great Lakes region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado was captured on video near its beginning. A television antenna tower near a home was knocked over. Otherwise, damage was limited to disturbed corn stubble in agricultural fields along U.S. Highway 41 north of Slaughters. Peak winds were estimated near 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Ohio River rose again around mid-month as a pair of strong low pressure systems lifted northeast from the Plains to the Great Lakes region. The first system produced widespread heavy rain on the 9th, followed by another heavy rainfall event on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Ohio River. Low-lying woods and farmland near the river were inundated. A few rural roads were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed near a surface low over central Missouri during the warmer and more unstable afternoon hours of the 24th. Although these storms weakened before reaching western Kentucky, a few of the storms produced hail.

Moderate to major river flooding subsided in March. The rainy weather pattern that dominated January and February became less active in March. The winter of 2018-19 ended up being the fourth wettest winter on record at Paducah, where records go back to 1937. February was the second wettest February on record at Paducah. Record rainfall in the Cumberland and Tennessee Valleys sent a large volume of water into Kentucky and Barkley Lakes. Both lakes crested around 367 feet, 8 feet above their summer pool stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The level of man-made Kentucky Lake was raised to 367 feet, about 8 feet above the normal summer pool stage. Low-lying parks, campgrounds, and marina parking lots near the lake were flooded. Some lake access roads were underwater. The lake level was still several feet below the record set during the spring of 2011 flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Recent heavy rainfall and slow discharge from Buckhorn Lake resulted in continued flooding in and around the town of Confluence from the first of the month through March 17. This led to several homes continuing to be surrounded by flood waters. Nearby roadways, including Kentucky Highway 257, also experienced inundation into the middle of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media, dispatch, and Emergency Management continued to report flooding issues in Confluence. Water inundation began back on February 21 as continued rounds of rainfall fell on saturated soils. Additionally, Buckhorn Lake rising to near record pool stage contributed to high water and flooding in Confluence as water was slowly discharged from Buckhorn Lake. Several homes and roads, including Kentucky Highway 257, were some of the entities that experienced inundation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds developed from Lake Cumberland into the Bluegrass region, as well as farther east to near Salyersville this afternoon and early evening. Damage included several downed trees, one of these falling onto a house near Somerset. An out building was also destroyed in Fleming County. Multiple rounds of heavy rain led to an instance of flash flooding near Somerset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported trees blown down along Kentucky Highway 2019 northwest of Salyersville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed during the mid afternoon hours, with a few of these reaching severe limits and subsequently resulting in wind damage. The worst of this occurred in Bell County near Pineville where large trees were blown onto several vehicles. A few homes and buildings were also damaged. As a result of the damage, the city's Independence Day Festival was cancelled.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported trees downed on the southwest side of West Liberty. A power pole near Morgan Central Elementary School was hit by some of these trees. A vehicle was also hit by falling trees, while the roof of a house was partially torn off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A round of thunderstorms developed during the mid to late afternoon hours on the 7th, resulting in flash flooding across segments of Knox and Bell Counties. Near Kayjay and Wheeler in Knox County, flowing water inundated roads and entered residences. Near Ingram in Bell County, homeowners were trapped as a footbridge was washed away.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported flash flooding from Kayjay Camp Road in Kayjay to Jonestown Road near Wheeler. Both roads were impassable as water entered residences.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported flowing water around Oat Field Branch off of Kentucky Highway 92 southwest of Ingram. This resulted in homeowners being trapped as a footbridge washed away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed this afternoon and evening. Due to heavy rainfall the previous day, combined with an additional one to two inches this afternoon, a portion of southern Bath County saw localized flash flooding. A thunderstorm in western Pike County also damaged a small tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported water flowing over the road at the intersection of Pine Grove Road and Pistol Road southeast of Olympia. Impacts included a culvert being washed out as well as the intersection being closed for a brief period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms intensified in coverage and intensity during the evening of July 17th, continuing into the early morning hours of July 18th. A couple of rounds of heavy rainfall occurred. One was near Kayjay in Knox County, where water entered residences and caused a road closure as 2.5 inches of rain fell. Bell County also experienced road impacts from swiftly flowing flood waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported swiftly flowing water moving across a rural road in the Meldrum community.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kentucky Highway 339 was closed near the Graves County line due to flooding of Mayfield Creek. Highways 994 and 1014 near Hendron were closed due to flooding of Champion Creek. Around three inches of rain fell during the pre-dawn hours, which brought creeks out of their banks.

Moderate to major river flooding subsided in March. The rainy weather pattern that dominated January and February became less active in March. The winter of 2018-19 ended up being the fourth wettest winter on record at Paducah, where records go back to 1937. February was the second wettest February on record at Paducah. Record rainfall in the Cumberland and Tennessee Valleys sent a large volume of water into Kentucky and Barkley Lakes. Both lakes crested around 367 feet, 8 feet above their summer pool stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged major river flood that peaked in February subsided in March. Information on the February crest height and the total property damage estimate is contained in the February report. The flood damaged about 50 homes in McCracken County. Levee and flood wall gates were installed in the Paducah area. This action along with heavy rainfall in west Kentucky and backwater from the Ohio and Tennessee Rivers caused widespread flooding in the southeast part of McCracken County. Smaller rivers and creeks such as the Clarks River had nowhere to drain and kept rising with each successive round of heavy rain. One fatality occurred due to a vehicle that drove into Clarks River floodwaters. A male, age 59, drove around a barricade and into floodwaters off Highway 787 near the Marshall County line. His girlfriend escaped the vehicle, but he did not. His body was recovered and CPR was administered but to no avail. Countywide, a damage assessment indicated 35 residences sustained minor damage and 15 sustained major damage. Numerous roads were closed around the county, especially in the flood plain west of Paducah and the Clarks River basin in eastern McCracken County. Some of the largest state roads that were closed included Highway 131 south of Reidland, Highway 305 in West Paducah, and Highway 284 between Reidland and Paducah. A Red Cross shelter was opened in Paducah. The City of Paducah declared a state of emergency and installed most of the floodgates in the floodwall. The city's convention center was inaccessible for over a week due to its location outside the floodwall. A makeshift barrier was installed around the convention center to prevent flooding.

Measured peak wind gusts at airport sites included 54 mph at Madisonville, 52 mph at Central City, 51 mph at Owensboro, 48 mph at Murray, 47 mph at Hopkinsville and Marion, and 46 mph at Paducah and Mayfield. In Daviess County, a tree fell on a house about four miles west of Philpot. In Muhlenberg County, trees were uprooted in Central City. In Calloway County, several tree branches were down in the Murray area. In Graves County, an old vacant building collapsed in downtown Mayfield. The area around the collapsed building was evacuated while a gas leak was capped off. In Marshall County, the strong winds contributed to a crash involving a semi on Interstate 24 on the night of the 13th. Strong crosswinds caused a semi truck and trailer to roll over, blocking both eastbound lanes near mile marker 21. Two other semis were unable to stop, and they struck the overturned semi. One driver was transported to a local hospital. The eastbound lanes were reopened about nine hours later.

A strong low pressure system produced multiple weather hazards. Scattered supercell thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front on the morning and early afternoon of the 14th, producing isolated wind damage, large hail, and several tornadoes. The supercells were preceded by widespread rain and thunderstorms the previous night, which caused some pockets of flooding in western Kentucky. The low pressure center tracked northeast across the Plains to the Great Lakes region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water rescues were conducted on Highway 1539 a few miles east of Henderson. The  creek flooding may have been aggravated by backwater from the Ohio River, which was near flood stage. Around two inches of rain fell during the predawn hours, causing flash flooding of creeks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system produced multiple weather hazards. Scattered supercell thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front on the morning and early afternoon of the 14th, producing isolated wind damage, large hail, and several tornadoes. The supercells were preceded by widespread rain and thunderstorms the previous night, which caused some pockets of flooding in western Kentucky. The low pressure center tracked northeast across the Plains to the Great Lakes region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This multi-county tornado first touched down near the intersection of Highways 1820 and 1628 in northeast Carlisle County. Only a very small portion of the track occurred in Carlisle County. The tornado proceeded northeast, passing mostly over undeveloped wetlands along Mayfield Creek. The damage path started with spotty tree damage and minor shingle damage. As the tornado approached the Ballard County line, a wider and more definitive path of snapped and uprooted trees became evident. Peak winds in Carlisle County were estimated near 105 mph as it was entering Ballard County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major river flooding subsided in March. The rainy weather pattern that dominated January and February became less active in March. The winter of 2018-19 ended up being the fourth wettest winter on record at Paducah, where records go back to 1937. February was the second wettest February on record at Paducah. Record rainfall in the Cumberland and Tennessee Valleys sent a large volume of water into Kentucky and Barkley Lakes. Both lakes crested around 367 feet, 8 feet above their summer pool stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding on the Mississippi River closed some roads. U.S. Highway 51 was closed near the Ballard/Carlisle County line due to backwater flooding of Mayfield Creek. Most of the other roads were county roads in the flood plain. The prolonged flooding started in February and continued into late March.

Record rainfall in the Cumberland and Tennessee Valleys sent a large volume of water into Kentucky and Barkley Lakes. Both lakes crested around 367 feet, 8 feet above their summer pool stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding of the Mississippi River inundated large areas of agricultural land. The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers assisted with the control of seepage through the levees near Hickman, where sand boils were a concern. Numerous county roads were closed, and about six state roads were closed in the flood plain. Most crops were not planted, so crop losses were small. The prolonged flooding started in February and continued into late March.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Along and northwest of a line from Owensboro to Murray, heat indices peaked between 101 and 107 degrees during the afternoon hours. A combination of temperatures in the mid 90's and dew points in the mid 70's produced these hazardous heat indices. A southwest wind flow of hot and humid air ahead of a cold front was responsible for this first potentially dangerous heat event of the season.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This multi-county tornado crossed extreme southeast Ballard County on its way from Carlisle County to McCracken County. The tornado track passed just northwest and north of the community of Lovelaceville. The two primary structural damage areas were the crossing points along Highway 1837 west of Lovelaceville and Hamburg Road north of Lovelaceville. West of Lovelaceville on Highway 1837, there was major roof damage to a few structures, including a mobile home and a farm outbuilding. A well-defined path of broken and uprooted trees was also evident. As the tornado reached Hamburg Road, the first evidence of EF-2 damage became visible. A large portion of the exterior wall of a house collapsed, and there was significant roof damage to the house. A nearby farm outbuilding was demolished. A swath of snapped and uprooted trees crossed the area. Building debris was deposited well downwind of the damaged or destroyed structures. Four cows were killed. The tornado path widened quickly, reaching 300 yards shortly after exiting Carlisle County.  Peak winds in Ballard County were estimated near 115 mph. The tornado then crossed into McCracken County, where the majority of the damage path was located.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system produced multiple weather hazards. Scattered supercell thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front on the morning and early afternoon of the 14th, producing isolated wind damage, large hail, and several tornadoes. The supercells were preceded by widespread rain and thunderstorms the previous night, which caused some pockets of flooding in western Kentucky. EVENT_NARRATIVE: This multi-county tornado entered McCracken County from Ballard County near the community of Lovelaceville. The tornado tracked within a couple of miles either side of U.S. Highway 62 for most of its path in McCracken County. The tornado intermittently reached EF-2 intensity from the Ballard County line to about two miles southeast of Barkley Regional Airport. A total of 25 dwellings were damaged along the path in McCracken County. Five businesses were damaged, and 20 residences were damaged. The damage was categorized as minor for 14 of the residences, major for one, and five were destroyed. Major structural damage included partial loss of roofs or walls. Minor damage to homes included the loss of shingles. Dozens of barns, outbuildings, garages, or grain bins were destroyed.  A church, located on U.S. Highway 62 near Highway 305, lost much of the roof structure as well as a large part of a second story exterior wall. This is where the tornado reached its maximum intensity, with estimated peak winds near 125 mph. A total of 40 pre-schoolers were inside a day care facility in the church, but none were directly injured. At least ten vehicles sustained damage in this area near the church. The only injury occurred on U.S. Highway 62 near the church, where a grain bin was blown on top of a pickup truck traveling eastbound. During the final few miles of its track, the tornado weakened as it moved through the western part of the city of Paducah. In the western part of Paducah, the tornado moved across U.S. Highway 60 and the city's main shopping mall. The tornado dissipated shortly after crossing Interstate 24 between exits 3 and 4. The roofs of the shopping mall and a nearby strip mall were damaged. Several businesses in the mall area and along Interstate 24 sustained minor damage, mostly to roofs. Windows were blown out of vehicles and buildings. A moving semi truck was lifted along Interstate 24 and was blown into the median in a jack-knife position. Along the entire track, dozens of utility poles were down, causing extensive power outages for nearly 10,000 customers. Hundreds of trees were snapped or uprooted. Video of the tornado was captured by personnel at the Paducah National Weather Service office, which the tornado missed by about two miles.

Severe storms developed across eastern Kentucky during the evening hours of June 5, 2019. These thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching frontal boundary.  The main threat early on was large hail.  An organized line of showers and storms then moved in from the north, producing more localized wind damage.||Significant hail up to 2 inches in diameter was reported in Wolfe County with the early storms.  There were also numerous trees blown down in areas, generally along and north of the Mountain Parkway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch relayed a report of a tree down along Kentucky Highway 211 south of Salt Lick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system swung up the Ohio River, leading to strengthening winds through the morning and afternoon into early on the 15th. These winds led to downed trees across portions of eastern Kentucky, mainly concentrated from the Bluegrass region down through the Interstate 75 corridor. Farther east, a portion of Kentucky Highway 160 near Hindman was shut down Sunday evening as downed trees resulted in power lines falling onto the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During a cold night with ongoing flooding from previous rains, a man died after driving his car around barricades and entering flood waters. The National Weather Service had messaged the flooding conditions for over a week prior to this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 9:00 pm CST on March 4, a male driver drove around a barricade before driving his car into standing water over the roadway. He called his girlfriend frantically stating he was in a river. That was the last anyone heard from him. His cell phone last pinged at 9:00 pm CST at a location just west of Interstate 65 north of the Tennessee border. The air temperature at the time was 19 degrees Fahrenheit.||His vehicle was found the next morning, and his body was found on Thursday March 7 a short distance from where his vehicle was found.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prolonged flooding of the Ohio River that began in February continued through the months of March and April. The April flooding was mainly in the Wickliffe area, extending as far upriver as the Oscar area. Much of the flooding was due to backwater from the swollen Mississippi River. The flooding during April was minor to moderate, which had a considerably smaller impact than the major flooding earlier in the season. The highest river level in April was 49.08 feet on the Cairo, IL river gage. This is still well above the flood stage of 40 feet. Considerable flooding of low-lying woods, fields, and a wildlife management area persisted through the month. The river flooding had an increasingly large impact on agricultural interests, since planting season begins in April. However, direct crop losses were still negligible due to the lack of planted crops.

An upper low over the northern Plains moved northeast toward the Great Lakes throughout the day, with an associated surface cold front draping south from the system. Ahead of the front a strong low level jet caused southern Indiana and central Kentucky to be placed under a Wind Advisory earlier in the day. As the front approached from the west, severe weather become more of an issue, with central Kentucky receiving large hail and structural damage from thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was observed near State Road 373.

As the front approached from the west, severe weather become more of an issue, with central Kentucky receiving large hail and structural damage from thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was reported in La Grange.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Green River and portions of the Ohio River rose above flood stage late in the month. The flooding was minor, consisting of flooded fields and woodlands in the flood plain. A few county roads were affected. From the 18th through the 20th, numerous showers and a few thunderstorms produced rain amounts of 1 to 3 inches. Deep southerly flow brought plenty of moisture northward for the heavy rainfall.

A cluster of storms formed across eastern Kentucky on the evening of the 20th as an upper level disturbance traveled through the area. Strong winds associated with these storms caused trees and power lines to come down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down along Route 1690 near the town of Cherryville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms developed across eastern Kentucky during the evening hours of June 5, 2019. These thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching frontal boundary.  The main threat early on was large hail.  An organized line of showers and storms then moved in from the north, producing more localized wind damage.||Significant hail up to 2 inches in diameter was reported in Wolfe County with the early storms.  There were also numerous trees blown down in areas, generally along and north of the Mountain Parkway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Employee observed golf ball sized hail while dispatch reported 2 inch diameter hail between Trent and Toliver.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog blanketed most of western Kentucky, except for the Henderson and Owensboro areas. Visibility was reduced to one-quarter mile or less for several hours. The fog formed just ahead of the leading edge of drier air that was marked by a weak cold front.

A line of thunderstorms moved east through the region ahead of an advancing cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn was collapsed and several outbuildings were damaged. In addition boat docks were tipped over and there was minor residential roof damage which consisted of  shingles and tin material being removed. Finally, numerous trees were knocked down and tree limbs snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rather pronounced upper-level shortwave trough moved eastward from the St. Louis area across the Ohio Valley in the afternoon. Daytime heating contributed to moderate instability, resulting in clusters of thunderstorms south of a weak stationary front from southern Illinois across southern Indiana. An isolated microburst accompanied one of the most intense storm clusters in northwest Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind was strong enough to move a barge on the Ohio River close to an industrial area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex tracked east across northwest Kentucky during the morning. The strongest storms produced some hail. A deep southwest wind flow through the depth of the atmosphere brought abundant moisture for storms. Precipitable water values were just over 1.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to nickel-size hail was reported in western Daviess County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms pummeled central portions of eastern Kentucky during the evening hours of May 26, 2019. These thunderstorms developed along a surface boundary, in tandem with a mesoscale convective vortex. These features helped to organize storms into a line, which swept across eastern Kentucky and produced several reports of damage.||Numerous trees were blown down from Rockcastle to Martin and Pike Counties. This subsequently led to thousands of power outages across eastern Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported trees were blown down all across the county, including across several roads near Brodhead as well as Mt. Vernon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a MCV (mesoscale convective vortex) over Arkansas, strong thunderstorms moved northeast from Tennessee into western Kentucky. Some of these storms produced dime to nickel-size hail. The storms weakened as they encountered more stable air along and north of a stationary front near the southern Kentucky border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off-duty meteorologist reported mostly one-quarter to one-half inch hailstones, but a few stones were up to three-quarters of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of heavy rain moved into Bell County on the evening of May 11th, following earlier rainfall. This led to a couple of mudslides, as well as minor flooding near poorly draining culverts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported a mudslide near the intersection of Kentucky Highways 1491 and 190 near Clear Creek Springs due to recent rounds of moderate to heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an upper level impulse and southward sagging frontal boundary out of the Ohio Valley brought a line of strong to severe thunderstorms across eastern Kentucky. The most intense of these storms moved through far eastern Kentucky, including Prestonsburg where a 70-80 mph downburst blew half the roof off of a hock shop building.||A man driving on West Court Street was crushed by this roof, resulting in his death. Damage stretched for about 1 mile through the city. Severe winds blew out a few of the windows of a 2 story building on West Court Street, while a large healthy poplar tree was uprooted.||Elsewhere, from West Liberty and Jackson eastward through Pike County, mainly tree damage was sustained. In Letcher County, a rotating supercell produced quarter to golf ball sized hail near Jenkins, while Somerset experienced isolated damage from a thunderstorm wind gust on the southwest flank of the line of storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported penny sized hail in Crockett.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed over the Pennyrile region of western Kentucky, producing large hail and damaging winds along its path. A cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms then formed in the Hopkinsville area, causing isolated tree damage and heavy rainfall. The storms formed during the very warm and unstable afternoon hours as a weakening cold front crossed northwest Missouri. A moderately strong southwest wind flow at and above 850 mb provided abundant moisture.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This large hail was reported southeast of Princeton along Kentucky Highway 91 near the Christian County line.

Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed this afternoon, continuing into the evening along an outflow boundary. Several of these storms momentarily became strong as this outflow boundary intersected them, with two reaching severe limits. One of these was just southwest of Corbin as it moved over the Oak Grove community, and the other was in Jackson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator observed a large tree blown down on Oak Grove School Road just southwest of Corbin.

The highest wind gust estimated was in downtown Prestonsburg on West Court Street |where one half of the roof of a hock shop building was lifted off. This roof landed on West Court Street, including on multiple vehicles, fatally wounding one person in a moving vehicle. Glass was also broken on multiple windows on the north side of the building. There were a number of other buildings and homes with minor roof |or gutter damage nearby, as well as a couple of uprooted trees near the downtown area. A HAM radio tower that had been mounted on the top of the annex building beside the jail and behind the courthouse in downtown Prestonsburg was also destroyed by thunderstorm wind gusts. The winds snapped the support wires that had been holding up the tower causing the tower to break in half and fall onto the roof of the building it had been mounted on and the ground next to the building. Wind speeds that uprooted a tree at a residence on South Highland Avenue were estimated to be in the upper end of the 70 to 80 mph range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed over the Pennyrile region of western Kentucky, producing large hail and damaging winds along its path. A cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms then formed in the Hopkinsville area, causing isolated tree damage and heavy rainfall. The storms formed during the very warm and unstable afternoon hours as a weakening cold front crossed northwest Missouri. A moderately strong southwest wind flow at and above 850 mb provided abundant moisture.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to ping-pong ball size hail was reported near Lake Malone on Kentucky Highway 1985.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of rain worked back into eastern Kentucky early on February 20 as a warm frontal boundary lifted north across the Commonwealth. A cold front brought additional rainfall through the afternoon and evening, before drier conditions worked into eastern Kentucky during the morning of February 21. ||Rain quickly spread back north that evening into the 22nd with southeastern Kentucky continuing to see prolonged light rain into the afternoon of the 23rd. A cold front brought additional rain to much of eastern Kentucky late on the 23rd. ||A spike in moisture ahead of this front resulted in additional moderate to heavy rain and high rainfall rates across portions of eastern Kentucky late on the 23rd. The convection ahead of the cold front that evening resulted in the majority of the flooding and flash flooding issues across eastern Kentucky. Showers and thunderstorms capable of heavy rainfall trained over parts of Wayne, Pulaski, Rockcastle, and other counties along the I-75 corridor, resulting in deadly flash flooding and river flooding along the Cumberland River. Later in the evening, the bulk of the rainfall shifted north to counties along the Mountain Parkway. Flash flooding and river flooding occurred in Powell, Wolfe, Breathitt, Magoffin, and Johnson Counties. Lighter rainfall continued into the night as flooding issues remained widespread into the 24th.||Rainfall amounts from this event ranged from near 2 inches in portions of the Bluegrass region and northeast Kentucky, to greater than 4 inches across locations in southern and southeast Kentucky. ||This additional water on top of saturated ground continued to promote mud and rock slides across eastern Kentucky, complete with power outages from fallen debris and trees. Pikeville experienced a massive rockslide early on February 22, resulting in several cars and a building being damaged. Other impacts included countless roads being inundated with standing water, pavement and water pipes breaking, water threatening homes, roads and bridges being washed away, and rising lake levels.||Of the 33 counties across eastern Kentucky, at least 27 were forced to declare State of Emergencies due to rainfall during and before this event. This included Knott County due to over $150,000 in county road damages. Beattyville, Clay City, Harlan, Hazard, Jenkins, Monticello, Paintsville, Pikeville, Salyersville, Stanton, West Liberty, and Whitesburg also declared State of Emergencies. Additionally, the Kentucky Governor declared a State of Emergency across the entire Commonwealth.||The persistent rainfall of the winter season and the amount of rainfall from this event led to Lake Cumberland reporting their highest lake level of 756.52 feet on February 26th, breaking the old record of 751.69 feet dating back to May 1984. As a result, Wolf Creek Dam had to release at a record discharge level of 59,880 cubic feet per second. Cave Run Lake also rose to within 6 feet of their record pool elevation. Buckhorn Lake came within 10 feet of their all-time record, partially resulting in Kentucky Highway 257 near Confluence having flood waters cover the highway isolating 40 to 50 families for three weeks following the end of the flooding rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public and an NWS employee reported numerous buildings in Campton being inundated with water. This includes the Chevrolet Dealership as well as a funeral home. Kentucky Highway 191 between Campton and Hazel Green was closed due to water running over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system strengthened as it moved northeast into the Great Lakes region. A large area of showers and thunderstorms associated with a warm front moved across the region during the afternoon of the 23rd, producing heavy rain and some flash flooding. The hardest-hit areas were in the Kentucky counties bordering Tennessee, including the city of Murray. From two to four inches of rain fell in the southern border counties of western Kentucky, mostly within a four-hour period. Behind a trailing cold front, west winds gusted from 40 to 50 mph late on the night of the 23rd into the early morning of the 24th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local news media outlet reported that a motorist crashed into Wolf Creek off of Stidham Road south of Cinda. The driver's body was later found trapped inside the car. Additionally, a bystander also died as a result of the flooded creek after he jumped in to try to save the driver. The creek was 3 to 4 times higher than normal, contributing to the death of both men.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another very moist storm system similar to the system on the 7th brought widespread heavy rainfall. Rainfall amounts from 2 to 4 inches were common during a 48-hour period. Isolated higher amounts near 5 inches occurred near the Tennessee border from Fulton to Murray. This heavy rain fell on ground that was saturated from previous heavy rain events earlier in the month, causing rapid runoff. A weak trough passed south across the area and then moved back north as a warm front as an area of low pressure developed in the southern Plains and moved east along the front. Widespread showers brought heavy downpours before a cold front moved east on the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of county roads were closed by high water in the western end of the county. On the north side of Elkton, Elk Fork Creek was out of its banks. Public safety officials  alerted nearby residents of the danger.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked east-northeast from the lower Mississippi Valley to the Carolinas. On the north side of this low, widespread rain changed to snow, sleet, and freezing rain on the afternoon and evening of the 15th. The precipitation was mostly in the form of freezing rain from the Land-Between-The-Lakes area westward, where isolated power outages and tree damage occurred. Power lines came down across U.S. Highway 62 near Gilbertsville in Marshall County, blocking both lanes. At a church near Dexter in Calloway County, an uprooted tree landed partially on an outdoor picnic pavilion. Around one-quarter inch of ice glazed trees and power lines across far western Kentucky, including the Paducah, Mayfield, Benton, and Murray areas. The highest reported ice accumulation was about 0.4 inch in Eddyville in Lyon County. Road pavement temperatures were slow to drop below freezing, but some slush and ice developed on roads before the precipitation ended. A single-vehicle accident on Interstate 24 over the Ohio River closed the westbound lanes for a short time. The metal-grating bridge over the Ohio River on U.S. 45 from Brookport to Paducah was closed due to icing. Elsewhere across western Kentucky, the wintry precipitation was mostly in the form of sleet and snow. Sleet and snow accumulations were generally an inch or less.

Moderate to major river flooding developed during the month. After a wet January, the active weather pattern continued into February. Frequent moderate to heavy rain events continued pushing not only the monthly and seasonal precipitation totals higher but also the rivers. The winter of 2018-19 ended up being the fourth wettest winter on record at Paducah, where records go back to 1937. February was the second wettest February on record at Paducah. The monthly rainfall total at Paducah was 10.42 inches. Persistent south to southwest winds aloft brought a steady supply of moisture from the Pacific or the Gulf of Mexico into the region. Fronts that lifted north across the region brought southerly winds and moisture into the lower levels. When the two coincided, areas of very heavy rain occurred. Record rainfall in the Cumberland and Tennessee Valleys sent a large volume of water into Kentucky and Barkley Lakes. Both lakes crested around 367 feet, 8 feet above their summer pool stage. For many locations on the lower Ohio River, it was the highest stage since the historic 2011 flood. The flood crest at Paducah was tied with the 1950 flood for the second highest since the Flood of 1937. The 2011 flood remains the worst flood since the Flood of 1937 at Paducah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding of the Ohio River occurred. At the Shawneetown, IL gage, the river crested at 48.96 feet on February 20. Flood stage there is 33 feet, and moderate flooding begins at 43 feet. The ferry from Hardin County, IL to Crittenden County was shut down due to high water for an extended period of time. Several state roads and numerous county roads were flooded. Backwater flooding along the Tradewater River affected areas near the Webster County line. Large areas of agricultural land were flooded. Crop losses were negligible since planting was not yet underway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 19, 2019, a broad upper trough dipped south to the Gulf of Mexico and carried abundant amounts of moisture towards the Ohio Valley. Once the moisture was transport was underway, isentropic lift caused 1.5 to 3 of rainfall along the Mississippi and Ohio River Valleys. The higher amounts went as far north as south central Indiana.||On the 20th, an upper trough axis and cold front pushed through southern Indiana and central Kentucky. The heaviest rain fell during the morning and into the afternoon hours before tapering off from west to east late on the 20th.||Moving into the 22nd, the upper flow amplified once again with a deep southwest flow aloft.  Isentropic lift was underway resulting in widespread light rain pushing northward from Tennessee into Kentucky during the day.  By that night, the ridge increased slightly across the region with a surface warm front pushing northward.  More moderate to heavy rainfall fell during the night which caused localized flooding. ||On the evening of the 23rd, surface low pressure in the vicinity of the Kansas City, MO area with an arcing cold front pushed towards the Mississippi River.  This cold front pushed through the region during the night and brought more moderate to heavy |rain along with some thunderstorms. ||All this rain and the saturated ground caused many flooding problems around central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kentucky 214 ferry was closed due to flooding.
