EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain produced a light glaze of ice on area roadways. Accumulations of ice were just a few hundredths of an inch. Along and north of a line from Marion to Madisonville to Central City, the ice had a major impact on the early morning commute. Numerous accidents were reported, which caused traffic snarls that hindered the ability of salt trucks to treat roads. Schools were closed in most areas, including Henderson, Daviess, Crittenden, Mclean, Hopkins, Muhlenberg, Union, and Webster Counties. The U.S. Highway 41 twin bridges over the Ohio River were closed due to the ice. Salt trucks were unable to treat the bridge due to a traffic snarl caused by an accident. The freezing rain was caused by a warm front that extended from west to east across the lower Ohio Valley.

Low-lying woodlands and fields in the river bottomlands were flooded. In Daviess County, about three dozen roads were closed by high water. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 19, 2019, a broad upper trough dipped south to the Gulf of Mexico and carried abundant amounts of moisture towards the Ohio Valley. Once the moisture was transport was underway, isentropic lift caused 1.5 to 3 of rainfall along the Mississippi and Ohio River Valleys. The higher amounts went as far north as south central Indiana.||On the 20th, an upper trough axis and cold front pushed through southern Indiana and central Kentucky. The heaviest rain fell during the morning and into the afternoon hours before tapering off from west to east late on the 20th.||Moving into the 22nd, the upper flow amplified once again with a deep southwest flow aloft.  Isentropic lift was underway resulting in widespread light rain pushing northward from Tennessee into Kentucky during the day.  By that night, the ridge increased slightly across the region with a surface warm front pushing northward.  More moderate to heavy rainfall fell during the night which caused localized flooding. ||On the evening of the 23rd, surface low pressure in the vicinity of the Kansas City, MO area with an arcing cold front pushed towards the Mississippi River.  This cold front pushed through the region during the night and brought more moderate to heavy |rain along with some thunderstorms. ||All this rain and the saturated ground caused many flooding problems around central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency services were en route to perform a swift water rescue on a stranded motorist on Salem Church Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain produced a light glaze of ice on area roadways. Accumulations of ice were just a few hundredths of an inch. Along and north of a line from Marion to Madisonville to Central City, the ice had a major impact on the early morning commute. Numerous accidents were reported, which caused traffic snarls that hindered the ability of salt trucks to treat roads. Schools were closed in most areas, including Henderson, Daviess, Crittenden, Mclean, Hopkins, Muhlenberg, Union, and Webster Counties. The U.S. Highway 41 twin bridges over the Ohio River were closed due to the ice. Salt trucks were unable to treat the bridge due to a traffic snarl caused by an accident. The freezing rain was caused by a warm front that extended from west to east across the lower Ohio Valley.

A low pressure system strengthened as it moved northeast into the Great Lakes region. A large area of showers and thunderstorms associated with a warm front moved across the region during the afternoon of the 23rd, producing heavy rain and some flash flooding. The hardest-hit areas were in the Kentucky counties bordering Tennessee, including the city of Murray. From two to four inches of rain fell in the southern border counties of western Kentucky, mostly within a four-hour period. Behind a trailing cold front, west winds gusted from 40 to 50 mph late on the night of the 23rd into the early morning of the 24th.

A low pressure system tracked east-northeast from the lower Mississippi Valley to the Carolinas. On the north side of this low, widespread rain changed to snow, sleet, and freezing rain on the afternoon and evening of the 15th. The precipitation was mostly in the form of freezing rain from the Land-Between-The-Lakes area westward, where isolated power outages and tree damage occurred. Power lines came down across U.S. Highway 62 near Gilbertsville in Marshall County, blocking both lanes. At a church near Dexter in Calloway County, an uprooted tree landed partially on an outdoor picnic pavilion. Around one-quarter inch of ice glazed trees and power lines across far western Kentucky, including the Paducah, Mayfield, Benton, and Murray areas. The highest reported ice accumulation was about 0.4 inch in Eddyville in Lyon County. Road pavement temperatures were slow to drop below freezing, but some slush and ice developed on roads before the precipitation ended. A single-vehicle accident on Interstate 24 over the Ohio River closed the westbound lanes for a short time. The metal-grating bridge over the Ohio River on U.S. 45 from Brookport to Paducah was closed due to icing. Elsewhere across western Kentucky, the wintry precipitation was mostly in the form of sleet and snow. Sleet and snow accumulations were generally an inch or less.

Moderate to major river flooding developed during the month. After a wet January, the active weather pattern continued into February. Frequent moderate to heavy rain events continued pushing not only the monthly and seasonal precipitation totals higher but also the rivers. The winter of 2018-19 ended up being the fourth wettest winter on record at Paducah, where records go back to 1937. February was the second wettest February on record at Paducah. The monthly rainfall total at Paducah was 10.42 inches. Persistent south to southwest winds aloft brought a steady supply of moisture from the Pacific or the Gulf of Mexico into the region. Fronts that lifted north across the region brought southerly winds and moisture into the lower levels. When the two coincided, areas of very heavy rain occurred. Record rainfall in the Cumberland and Tennessee Valleys sent a large volume of water into Kentucky and Barkley Lakes. Both lakes crested around 367 feet, 8 feet above their summer pool stage. For many locations on the lower Ohio River, it was the highest stage since the historic 2011 flood. The flood crest at Paducah was tied with the 1950 flood for the second highest since the Flood of 1937. The 2011 flood remains the worst flood since the Flood of 1937 at Paducah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred on the Ohio River. At the Owensboro river gage, the river crested at 44.5 feet on the 17th. Flood stage there is 40 feet. Low-lying woodlands and  fields in the river bottomlands were flooded. Numerous roads were flooded, including some state roads. Backwater from the Ohio River caused flooding near the mouth of the Green River, which flooded roads in eastern Henderson County. Flooded roads extended inland to near Hebbardsville.

The flood crest at Paducah was tied with the 1950 flood for the second highest since the Flood of 1937. The 2011 flood remains the worst flood since the Flood of 1937 at Paducah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding on the Mississippi River closed some roads. U.S. Highway 51 was closed near the Carlisle County line due to backwater flooding of Mayfield Creek. Most of the other roads were county roads in the flood plain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 19, 2019, a broad upper trough dipped south to the Gulf of Mexico and carried abundant amounts of moisture towards the Ohio Valley. Once the moisture was transport was underway, isentropic lift caused 1.5 to 3 of rainfall along the Mississippi and Ohio River Valleys. The higher amounts went as far north as south central Indiana.||On the 20th, an upper trough axis and cold front pushed through southern Indiana and central Kentucky. The heaviest rain fell during the morning and into the afternoon hours before tapering off from west to east late on the 20th.||Moving into the 22nd, the upper flow amplified once again with a deep southwest flow aloft.  Isentropic lift was underway resulting in widespread light rain pushing northward from Tennessee into Kentucky during the day.  By that night, the ridge increased slightly across the region with a surface warm front pushing northward.  More moderate to heavy rainfall fell during the night which caused localized flooding. ||On the evening of the 23rd, surface low pressure in the vicinity of the Kansas City, MO area with an arcing cold front pushed towards the Mississippi River.  This cold front pushed through the region during the night and brought more moderate to heavy |rain along with some thunderstorms. ||All this rain and the saturated ground caused many flooding problems around central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kentucky 2592 at Highway is impassable due to flooding. Highway 73 is still open.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked east-northeast from the lower Mississippi Valley to the Carolinas. On the north side of this low, widespread rain changed to snow, sleet, and freezing rain on the afternoon and evening of the 15th. The precipitation was mostly in the form of freezing rain from the Land-Between-The-Lakes area westward, where isolated power outages and tree damage occurred. Power lines came down across U.S. Highway 62 near Gilbertsville in Marshall County, blocking both lanes. At a church near Dexter in Calloway County, an uprooted tree landed partially on an outdoor picnic pavilion. Around one-quarter inch of ice glazed trees and power lines across far western Kentucky, including the Paducah, Mayfield, Benton, and Murray areas. The highest reported ice accumulation was about 0.4 inch in Eddyville in Lyon County. Road pavement temperatures were slow to drop below freezing, but some slush and ice developed on roads before the precipitation ended. A single-vehicle accident on Interstate 24 over the Ohio River closed the westbound lanes for a short time. The metal-grating bridge over the Ohio River on U.S. 45 from Brookport to Paducah was closed due to icing. Elsewhere across western Kentucky, the wintry precipitation was mostly in the form of sleet and snow. Sleet and snow accumulations were generally an inch or less.

Moderate to major river flooding developed during the month. After a wet January, the active weather pattern continued into February. Frequent moderate to heavy rain events continued pushing not only the monthly and seasonal precipitation totals higher but also the rivers. The winter of 2018-19 ended up being the fourth wettest winter on record at Paducah, where records go back to 1937. February was the second wettest February on record at Paducah. The monthly rainfall total at Paducah was 10.42 inches. Persistent south to southwest winds aloft brought a steady supply of moisture from the Pacific or the Gulf of Mexico into the region. Fronts that lifted north across the region brought southerly winds and moisture into the lower levels. When the two coincided, areas of very heavy rain occurred. Record rainfall in the Cumberland and Tennessee Valleys sent a large volume of water into Kentucky and Barkley Lakes. Both lakes crested around 367 feet, 8 feet above their summer pool stage. For many locations on the lower Ohio River, it was the highest stage since the historic 2011 flood. The flood crest at Paducah was tied with the 1950 flood for the second highest since the Flood of 1937. The 2011 flood remains the worst flood since the Flood of 1937 at Paducah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding on the Mississippi River closed some roads. Most of the roads were county roads in the flood plain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of showers, including embedded thunderstorms, worked across eastern Kentucky from February 6th into the 7th along a slow-moving frontal boundary. A thunderstorm briefly became severe near and north of Somerset, producing tree damage. Gusty winds continued throughout the evening of the 7th, which when combined with saturated soils, led to a tree falling onto a mobile home in Laurel County. Rainfall amounts near two inches fell across portions of southeast Kentucky, leading to standing water and a few rock/mud slides. These produced road closures in Harlan, Knott, and Perry Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Department of Highway officials reported that Kentucky Highway 72 was blocked between Kentucky Highway 2425 and Pruitt's Curve due to a large rock slide. Crews were able to open one lane by late morning, following the Harlan County Judge Executive declaring a State of Emergency. This was likely caused by 1.5 to 2 inches of rain on top of saturated terrain. Crews worked to clean up the slide for the next 5 weeks, causing travel impacts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of rain worked back into eastern Kentucky early on February 20 as a warm frontal boundary lifted north across the Commonwealth. A cold front brought additional rainfall through the afternoon and evening, before drier conditions worked into eastern Kentucky during the morning of February 21. ||Rain quickly spread back north that evening into the 22nd with southeastern Kentucky continuing to see prolonged light rain into the afternoon of the 23rd. A cold front brought additional rain to much of eastern Kentucky late on the 23rd. ||A spike in moisture ahead of this front resulted in additional moderate to heavy rain and high rainfall rates across portions of eastern Kentucky late on the 23rd. The convection ahead of the cold front that evening resulted in the majority of the flooding and flash flooding issues across eastern Kentucky. Showers and thunderstorms capable of heavy rainfall trained over parts of Wayne, Pulaski, Rockcastle, and other counties along the I-75 corridor, resulting in deadly flash flooding and river flooding along the Cumberland River. Later in the evening, the bulk of the rainfall shifted north to counties along the Mountain Parkway. Flash flooding and river flooding occurred in Powell, Wolfe, Breathitt, Magoffin, and Johnson Counties. Lighter rainfall continued into the night as flooding issues remained widespread into the 24th.||Rainfall amounts from this event ranged from near 2 inches in portions of the Bluegrass region and northeast Kentucky, to greater than 4 inches across locations in southern and southeast Kentucky. ||This additional water on top of saturated ground continued to promote mud and rock slides across eastern Kentucky, complete with power outages from fallen debris and trees. Pikeville experienced a massive rockslide early on February 22, resulting in several cars and a building being damaged. Other impacts included countless roads being inundated with standing water, pavement and water pipes breaking, water threatening homes, roads and bridges being washed away, and rising lake levels.||Of the 33 counties across eastern Kentucky, at least 27 were forced to declare State of Emergencies due to rainfall during and before this event. This included Knott County due to over $150,000 in county road damages. Beattyville, Clay City, Harlan, Hazard, Jenkins, Monticello, Paintsville, Pikeville, Salyersville, Stanton, West Liberty, and Whitesburg also declared State of Emergencies. Additionally, the Kentucky Governor declared a State of Emergency across the entire Commonwealth.||The persistent rainfall of the winter season and the amount of rainfall from this event led to Lake Cumberland reporting their highest lake level of 756.52 feet on February 26th, breaking the old record of 751.69 feet dating back to May 1984. As a result, Wolf Creek Dam had to release at a record discharge level of 59,880 cubic feet per second. Cave Run Lake also rose to within 6 feet of their record pool elevation. Buckhorn Lake came within 10 feet of their all-time record, partially resulting in Kentucky Highway 257 near Confluence having flood waters cover the highway isolating 40 to 50 families for three weeks following the end of the flooding rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Department of Highway officials reported a pavement break, resulting from recent heavy rain, on Kentucky Highway 167 north of the Tennessee state line. The road was reduced to one lane for several days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked east-northeast from the lower Mississippi Valley to the Carolinas. On the north side of this low, widespread rain changed to snow, sleet, and freezing rain on the afternoon and evening of the 15th. The precipitation was mostly in the form of freezing rain from the Land-Between-The-Lakes area westward, where isolated power outages and tree damage occurred. Power lines came down across U.S. Highway 62 near Gilbertsville in Marshall County, blocking both lanes. At a church near Dexter in Calloway County, an uprooted tree landed partially on an outdoor picnic pavilion. Around one-quarter inch of ice glazed trees and power lines across far western Kentucky, including the Paducah, Mayfield, Benton, and Murray areas. The highest reported ice accumulation was about 0.4 inch in Eddyville in Lyon County. Road pavement temperatures were slow to drop below freezing, but some slush and ice developed on roads before the precipitation ended. A single-vehicle accident on Interstate 24 over the Ohio River closed the westbound lanes for a short time. The metal-grating bridge over the Ohio River on U.S. 45 from Brookport to Paducah was closed due to icing. Elsewhere across western Kentucky, the wintry precipitation was mostly in the form of sleet and snow. Sleet and snow accumulations were generally an inch or less.

A warm front moved slowly northward from the Gulf Coast states on the night of the 19th, reaching the lower Ohio Valley on the morning of the 20th. This warm front brought the third widespread heavy rainfall event so far in February. Rainfall totals over a 24-hour period were from 2 to 3 inches in much of western Kentucky, with locally up to 3.5 inches west of Kentucky Lake. This rainfall fell on moist ground, causing many creeks and smaller rivers to flood. The flooding was exacerbated by very high levels on the Ohio and Mississippi Rivers, which caused tributary rivers and creeks to back up into western Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two vehicles were stalled in floodwaters at a railroad underpass in Clinton. Multiple highways were covered by water around the county, especially near flood-prone creeks.

A few  state roads near these flood-prone creeks were closed. These roads included Routes 994 and 1014 at Champion Creek and Route 339 at Mayfield Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 19, 2019, a broad upper trough dipped south to the Gulf of Mexico and carried abundant amounts of moisture towards the Ohio Valley. Once the moisture was transport was underway, isentropic lift caused 1.5 to 3 of rainfall along the Mississippi and Ohio River Valleys. The higher amounts went as far north as south central Indiana.||On the 20th, an upper trough axis and cold front pushed through southern Indiana and central Kentucky. The heaviest rain fell during the morning and into the afternoon hours before tapering off from west to east late on the 20th.||Moving into the 22nd, the upper flow amplified once again with a deep southwest flow aloft.  Isentropic lift was underway resulting in widespread light rain pushing northward from Tennessee into Kentucky during the day.  By that night, the ridge increased slightly across the region with a surface warm front pushing northward.  More moderate to heavy rainfall fell during the night which caused localized flooding. ||On the evening of the 23rd, surface low pressure in the vicinity of the Kansas City, MO area with an arcing cold front pushed towards the Mississippi River.  This cold front pushed through the region during the night and brought more moderate to heavy |rain along with some thunderstorms. ||All this rain and the saturated ground caused many flooding problems around central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hall Gaddie was closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked east-northeast from the lower Mississippi Valley to the Carolinas. On the north side of this low, widespread rain changed to snow, sleet, and freezing rain on the afternoon and evening of the 15th. The precipitation was mostly in the form of freezing rain from the Land-Between-The-Lakes area westward, where isolated power outages and tree damage occurred. Power lines came down across U.S. Highway 62 near Gilbertsville in Marshall County, blocking both lanes. At a church near Dexter in Calloway County, an uprooted tree landed partially on an outdoor picnic pavilion. Around one-quarter inch of ice glazed trees and power lines across far western Kentucky, including the Paducah, Mayfield, Benton, and Murray areas. The highest reported ice accumulation was about 0.4 inch in Eddyville in Lyon County. Road pavement temperatures were slow to drop below freezing, but some slush and ice developed on roads before the precipitation ended. A single-vehicle accident on Interstate 24 over the Ohio River closed the westbound lanes for a short time. The metal-grating bridge over the Ohio River on U.S. 45 from Brookport to Paducah was closed due to icing. Elsewhere across western Kentucky, the wintry precipitation was mostly in the form of sleet and snow. Sleet and snow accumulations were generally an inch or less.

A line of thunderstorms developed early in the morning from western Arkansas into south central Missouri ahead of a strong cold front. This line of storms intensified as it moved east-northeast across the mid Mississippi Valley later in the morning. Some severe weather continued through the midday hours as the line progressed up the lower Ohio Valley. This line of storms was the final round of heavy rain, which capped a very wet couple days that resulted in storm totals of 2 to 4 inches. Isolated higher amounts up to near 5 inches were reported. Isolated flooding was reported in some areas. Although instability was weak, very strong winds aloft facilitated isolated severe storms and a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down on Highway 1420 in the Ohio River flood plain. Power lines were also down on Highway 60 near downtown Paducah.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service revealed a short path of tornado damage a few miles northwest of the community of Robards. The maximum estimated winds were near 100 mph. A metal outbuilding completely lost its roof, with walls partially collapsed. One large tree trunk was snapped. Roofing material was lofted quite a distance into trees. Other minor tree damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system strengthened as it moved northeast into the Great Lakes region. A large area of showers and thunderstorms associated with a warm front moved across the region during the afternoon of the 23rd, producing heavy rain and some flash flooding. The hardest-hit areas were in the Kentucky counties bordering Tennessee, including the city of Murray. From two to four inches of rain fell in the southern border counties of western Kentucky, mostly within a four-hour period. Behind a trailing cold front, west winds gusted from 40 to 50 mph late on the night of the 23rd into the early morning of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding of creeks and roads was reported. The flooding occurred in locations that do not typically flood. Highway 893 was flooded near Lynn Grove. Numerous streets were flooded in and near Murray, especially in the vicinity of Bee Creek. A water rescue was conducted where a vehicle stalled out in floodwater north of Murray State University on North 16th Street. A few inches of water entered a residence. Near Kirksey, water was up to the bridge that crosses the west fork of Rock House Creek on Airport Road just south of Highway 464. In the eastern part of the county just north of Hamlin, one home was evacuated due to floodwaters on Highway 732. About 17 to 18 county roads were under water. The storm total rainfall from the Kentucky mesonet site at Murray was 3.66 inches, of which 2.56 inches fell in four hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over Highway 1175. Several other state and county roads were closed, including a road that crosses the Little River. One roadway was partially washed out. A trained spotter in Rockcastle measured a 48-hour total of 4.20 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 19, 2019, a broad upper trough dipped south to the Gulf of Mexico and carried abundant amounts of moisture towards the Ohio Valley. Once the moisture was transport was underway, isentropic lift caused 1.5 to 3 of rainfall along the Mississippi and Ohio River Valleys. The higher amounts went as far north as south central Indiana.||On the 20th, an upper trough axis and cold front pushed through southern Indiana and central Kentucky. The heaviest rain fell during the morning and into the afternoon hours before tapering off from west to east late on the 20th.||Moving into the 22nd, the upper flow amplified once again with a deep southwest flow aloft.  Isentropic lift was underway resulting in widespread light rain pushing northward from Tennessee into Kentucky during the day.  By that night, the ridge increased slightly across the region with a surface warm front pushing northward.  More moderate to heavy rainfall fell during the night which caused localized flooding. ||On the evening of the 23rd, surface low pressure in the vicinity of the Kansas City, MO area with an arcing cold front pushed towards the Mississippi River.  This cold front pushed through the region during the night and brought more moderate to heavy |rain along with some thunderstorms. ||All this rain and the saturated ground caused many flooding problems around central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kentucky 2785 was closed due to high water between mile markers 2.5 and 2.6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked east-northeast from the lower Mississippi Valley to the Carolinas. On the north side of this low, widespread rain changed to snow, sleet, and freezing rain on the afternoon and evening of the 15th. The precipitation was mostly in the form of freezing rain from the Land-Between-The-Lakes area westward, where isolated power outages and tree damage occurred. Power lines came down across U.S. Highway 62 near Gilbertsville in Marshall County, blocking both lanes. At a church near Dexter in Calloway County, an uprooted tree landed partially on an outdoor picnic pavilion. Around one-quarter inch of ice glazed trees and power lines across far western Kentucky, including the Paducah, Mayfield, Benton, and Murray areas. The highest reported ice accumulation was about 0.4 inch in Eddyville in Lyon County. Road pavement temperatures were slow to drop below freezing, but some slush and ice developed on roads before the precipitation ended. A single-vehicle accident on Interstate 24 over the Ohio River closed the westbound lanes for a short time. The metal-grating bridge over the Ohio River on U.S. 45 from Brookport to Paducah was closed due to icing. Elsewhere across western Kentucky, the wintry precipitation was mostly in the form of sleet and snow. Sleet and snow accumulations were generally an inch or less.

Moderate to major river flooding developed during the month. After a wet January, the active weather pattern continued into February. Frequent moderate to heavy rain events continued pushing not only the monthly and seasonal precipitation totals higher but also the rivers. The winter of 2018-19 ended up being the fourth wettest winter on record at Paducah, where records go back to 1937. February was the second wettest February on record at Paducah. The monthly rainfall total at Paducah was 10.42 inches. Persistent south to southwest winds aloft brought a steady supply of moisture from the Pacific or the Gulf of Mexico into the region. Fronts that lifted north across the region brought southerly winds and moisture into the lower levels. When the two coincided, areas of very heavy rain occurred. Record rainfall in the Cumberland and Tennessee Valleys sent a large volume of water into Kentucky and Barkley Lakes. Both lakes crested around 367 feet, 8 feet above their summer pool stage. For many locations on the lower Ohio River, it was the highest stage since the historic 2011 flood. The flood crest at Paducah was tied with the 1950 flood for the second highest since the Flood of 1937. The 2011 flood remains the worst flood since the Flood of 1937 at Paducah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged major river flood damaged about 50 homes in McCracken County. At Paducah, the Ohio River crested at 53.27 feet on the 25th. This was tied with the 1950 flood for the second worst flood since the Flood of 1937. Since 1937, only the flood crest of 2011 was higher. The flood crest reached 60.60 feet in the winter of 1937. Levee and flood wall gates were installed in the Paducah area. This action along with heavy rainfall in west Kentucky and backwater from the Ohio and Tennessee Rivers caused widespread flooding in the southeast part of McCracken County. Smaller rivers and creeks such as the Clarks River had nowhere to drain and kept rising with each successive round of heavy rain. A damage assessment indicated 35 residences sustained minor damage and 15 sustained major damage. Numerous roads were closed around the county, especially in the flood plain west of Paducah and the Clarks River basin in eastern McCracken County. Some of the largest state roads that were closed included Highway 131 south of Reidland, Highway 305 in West Paducah, and Highway 284 between Reidland and Paducah. A Red Cross shelter was opened in Paducah. The City of Paducah declared a state of emergency and installed most of the floodgates in the floodwall. The city's convention center was inaccessible for over a week due to its location outside the floodwall. A makeshift barrier was installed around the convention center to prevent flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system strengthened as it moved northeast into the Great Lakes region. A large area of showers and thunderstorms associated with a warm front moved across the region during the afternoon of the 23rd, producing heavy rain and some flash flooding. The hardest-hit areas were in the Kentucky counties bordering Tennessee, including the city of Murray. From two to four inches of rain fell in the southern border counties of western Kentucky, mostly within a four-hour period. Behind a trailing cold front, west winds gusted from 40 to 50 mph late on the night of the 23rd into the early morning of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several miles south of Hopkinsville, a water rescue was conducted on a flooded section of Millers Mill Road. A mother and child became stranded in a vehicle on the flooded road. There were no injuries. Elsewhere, minor flooding occurred along the Little River on the east side of Hopkinsville. Some low-lying woods and fields were inundated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water threatened a church and a couple of homes in Elkton. Water covered portions of State Roads 181, 848, and 475, as well as several county roads. Numerous roads were impassable due to high water. A mesonet site five miles southwest of Elkton measured a storm total of 3.01 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 19, 2019, a broad upper trough dipped south to the Gulf of Mexico and carried abundant amounts of moisture towards the Ohio Valley. Once the moisture was transport was underway, isentropic lift caused 1.5 to 3 of rainfall along the Mississippi and Ohio River Valleys. The higher amounts went as far north as south central Indiana.||On the 20th, an upper trough axis and cold front pushed through southern Indiana and central Kentucky. The heaviest rain fell during the morning and into the afternoon hours before tapering off from west to east late on the 20th.||Moving into the 22nd, the upper flow amplified once again with a deep southwest flow aloft.  Isentropic lift was underway resulting in widespread light rain pushing northward from Tennessee into Kentucky during the day.  By that night, the ridge increased slightly across the region with a surface warm front pushing northward.  More moderate to heavy rainfall fell during the night which caused localized flooding. ||On the evening of the 23rd, surface low pressure in the vicinity of the Kansas City, MO area with an arcing cold front pushed towards the Mississippi River.  This cold front pushed through the region during the night and brought more moderate to heavy |rain along with some thunderstorms. ||All this rain and the saturated ground caused many flooding problems around central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pictures showed that roads in Garrard County were impassible due to standing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major river flooding developed during the month. After a wet January, the active weather pattern continued into February. Frequent moderate to heavy rain events continued pushing not only the monthly and seasonal precipitation totals higher but also the rivers. The winter of 2018-19 ended up being the fourth wettest winter on record at Paducah, where records go back to 1937. February was the second wettest February on record at Paducah. The monthly rainfall total at Paducah was 10.42 inches. Persistent south to southwest winds aloft brought a steady supply of moisture from the Pacific or the Gulf of Mexico into the region. Fronts that lifted north across the region brought southerly winds and moisture into the lower levels. When the two coincided, areas of very heavy rain occurred. Record rainfall in the Cumberland and Tennessee Valleys sent a large volume of water into Kentucky and Barkley Lakes. Both lakes crested around 367 feet, 8 feet above their summer pool stage. For many locations on the lower Ohio River, it was the highest stage since the historic 2011 flood. The flood crest at Paducah was tied with the 1950 flood for the second highest since the Flood of 1937. The 2011 flood remains the worst flood since the Flood of 1937 at Paducah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged major river flood affected numerous homes and businesses in Livingston County. At Smithland, the Ohio River crested at 51.83 feet on the 25th. This was the worst flood since the historic flood of May 2011, when the river crested at 54.89 feet. The 1997 flood crest of 51.44 feet was similar to this crest. The primary focus of flood-fighting efforts revolved around the town of Smithland, the county seat. A makeshift levee was built along the river, requiring a multi-week closure of U.S. Highway 60 through town. The governor of Kentucky toured the site of the levee construction, which required the assistance of numerous state and federal agencies. Repeated heavy rainfall in west Kentucky and backwater from the Cumberland and Tennessee Rivers caused widespread flooding in the southern part of Livingston County. Smaller rivers and creeks had nowhere to drain and kept rising with each successive round of heavy rain. Numerous roads were closed around the county, especially in the flood plain. Some of the largest state roads that were closed included U.S. Highway 60 in Smithland and State Route 70. Floodwaters came right up to the edge of U.S. Highway 60 at several locations from Ledbetter to Burna, but the road was only closed briefly when the river crested. The closure of Highway 60 outside Smithland prompted the closure of Livingston County schools for a day. The county school district estimated 50 to 60 students were directly impacted by the floodwaters. The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers stopped all river traffic on the Ohio River at Smithland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 19, 2019, a broad upper trough dipped south to the Gulf of Mexico and carried abundant amounts of moisture towards the Ohio Valley. Once the moisture was transport was underway, isentropic lift caused 1.5 to 3 of rainfall along the Mississippi and Ohio River Valleys. The higher amounts went as far north as south central Indiana.||On the 20th, an upper trough axis and cold front pushed through southern Indiana and central Kentucky. The heaviest rain fell during the morning and into the afternoon hours before tapering off from west to east late on the 20th.||Moving into the 22nd, the upper flow amplified once again with a deep southwest flow aloft.  Isentropic lift was underway resulting in widespread light rain pushing northward from Tennessee into Kentucky during the day.  By that night, the ridge increased slightly across the region with a surface warm front pushing northward.  More moderate to heavy rainfall fell during the night which caused localized flooding. ||On the evening of the 23rd, surface low pressure in the vicinity of the Kansas City, MO area with an arcing cold front pushed towards the Mississippi River.  This cold front pushed through the region during the night and brought more moderate to heavy |rain along with some thunderstorms. ||All this rain and the saturated ground caused many flooding problems around central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a water rescue on Tates Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another very moist storm system similar to the system on the 7th brought widespread heavy rainfall. Rainfall amounts from 2 to 4 inches were common during a 48-hour period. Isolated higher amounts near 5 inches occurred near the Tennessee border from Fulton to Murray. This heavy rain fell on ground that was saturated from previous heavy rain events earlier in the month, causing rapid runoff. A weak trough passed south across the area and then moved back north as a warm front as an area of low pressure developed in the southern Plains and moved east along the front. Widespread showers brought heavy downpours before a cold front moved east on the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of state roads were closed due to flooding in Daviess County, including Highway 298 at mile marker 2 and Highway 762 at the Ohio County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major river flooding developed during the month. After a wet January, the active weather pattern continued into February. Frequent moderate to heavy rain events continued pushing not only the monthly and seasonal precipitation totals higher but also the rivers. The winter of 2018-19 ended up being the fourth wettest winter on record at Paducah, where records go back to 1937. February was the second wettest February on record at Paducah. The monthly rainfall total at Paducah was 10.42 inches. Persistent south to southwest winds aloft brought a steady supply of moisture from the Pacific or the Gulf of Mexico into the region. Fronts that lifted north across the region brought southerly winds and moisture into the lower levels. When the two coincided, areas of very heavy rain occurred. Record rainfall in the Cumberland and Tennessee Valleys sent a large volume of water into Kentucky and Barkley Lakes. Both lakes crested around 367 feet, 8 feet above their summer pool stage. For many locations on the lower Ohio River, it was the highest stage since the historic 2011 flood. The flood crest at Paducah was tied with the 1950 flood for the second highest since the Flood of 1937. The 2011 flood remains the worst flood since the Flood of 1937 at Paducah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flooding on the Ohio River closed numerous county roads and a few state roads.  U.S. Highway 60/62 was closed from Wickliffe to the Ohio River bridge to Cairo, Illinois. This road serves as a primary link between Kentucky and Illinois. A detour of about 80 miles was required for commuters in the area. Many hunting cabins in the river bottoms were flooded, but only a small number of houses (fewer than 20) were affected. The Ballard Wildlife Management Area was closed. The Ohio River crested at 53.27 feet at Paducah on the 25th. This was the highest crest since the historic 2011 flood, and tied for the second highest crest since the Flood of 1937 at Paducah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 19, 2019, a broad upper trough dipped south to the Gulf of Mexico and carried abundant amounts of moisture towards the Ohio Valley. Once the moisture was transport was underway, isentropic lift caused 1.5 to 3 of rainfall along the Mississippi and Ohio River Valleys. The higher amounts went as far north as south central Indiana.||On the 20th, an upper trough axis and cold front pushed through southern Indiana and central Kentucky. The heaviest rain fell during the morning and into the afternoon hours before tapering off from west to east late on the 20th.||Moving into the 22nd, the upper flow amplified once again with a deep southwest flow aloft.  Isentropic lift was underway resulting in widespread light rain pushing northward from Tennessee into Kentucky during the day.  By that night, the ridge increased slightly across the region with a surface warm front pushing northward.  More moderate to heavy rainfall fell during the night which caused localized flooding. ||On the evening of the 23rd, surface low pressure in the vicinity of the Kansas City, MO area with an arcing cold front pushed towards the Mississippi River.  This cold front pushed through the region during the night and brought more moderate to heavy |rain along with some thunderstorms. ||All this rain and the saturated ground caused many flooding problems around central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads were reported to be flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of gusty showers associated with a cold front crossed through Greenup County on the evening of the 12th. As a result of strong winds accompanying these showers, a tree fell on top of a mobile home at the intersection of KY-827 and KY-7, trapping residents of the home inside. First responders were called to the scene to cut the tree and free the trapped residents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of gusty showers associated with a cold front crossed through Greenup County on the evening of the 12th. As a result of strong winds accompanying these showers, a tree fell on top of a mobile home at the intersection of KY-827 and KY-7 and trapping residents of the home inside. First responders were called to the scene to cut the tree and free the trapped residents safely.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved out of the Plains into the Great Lakes on the 19th and 20th. Initially the precipitation started as a wintry mix, but accumulations were minor. Warmer air moved into the region through the night, with periods of moderate to heavy rainfall into the afternoon of the 20th. Rainfall amounts ranged from 1 to 1.5 inches. This rain fell on already saturated soil and led to minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads were closed due to flooding. This included Walker Lane due to high water on the East Fork of Little Sandy River and Route 854 along Rush Creek. Green Springer Road was closed along Williams Creek and a section of Route 5 was closed near Westwood due to flooding on Hood Creek.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed due to flooding. This included the intersection of State Routes 7 and 784 due to water spilling out of Tygarts Creek. Near Greenup, 2nd Street and the intersection of State Route 1 and Tanyard Hollow Road were closed due to flooding along Town Branch. The 3800 block of State Route 207 was also closed as the Little Sandy River overflowed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted northward into Kentucky on the evening of the 23rd, promoting widespread showers and a few isolated thunderstorms due to the close proximity of an approaching cold front. Almost two inches of rain fell between the evening of the 23rd and the morning of the 24th.||Behind the cold front, very gusty winds developed with most areas seeing gusts of 40 to 50 knots. Combined with the soggy ground, these winds led to power outages due to downed trees and power lines. Nearly a thousand power customers lost power, and it took a couple days for all service to be restored.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in flooding on Route 581 along Levisa Fork.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of rain worked back into eastern Kentucky early on February 20 as a warm frontal boundary lifted north across the Commonwealth. A cold front brought additional rainfall through the afternoon and evening, before drier conditions worked into eastern Kentucky during the morning of February 21. ||Rain quickly spread back north that evening into the 22nd with southeastern Kentucky continuing to see prolonged light rain into the afternoon of the 23rd. A cold front brought additional rain to much of eastern Kentucky late on the 23rd. ||A spike in moisture ahead of this front resulted in additional moderate to heavy rain and high rainfall rates across portions of eastern Kentucky late on the 23rd. The convection ahead of the cold front that evening resulted in the majority of the flooding and flash flooding issues across eastern Kentucky. Showers and thunderstorms capable of heavy rainfall trained over parts of Wayne, Pulaski, Rockcastle, and other counties along the I-75 corridor, resulting in deadly flash flooding and river flooding along the Cumberland River. Later in the evening, the bulk of the rainfall shifted north to counties along the Mountain Parkway. Flash flooding and river flooding occurred in Powell, Wolfe, Breathitt, Magoffin, and Johnson Counties. Lighter rainfall continued into the night as flooding issues remained widespread into the 24th.||Rainfall amounts from this event ranged from near 2 inches in portions of the Bluegrass region and northeast Kentucky, to greater than 4 inches across locations in southern and southeast Kentucky. ||This additional water on top of saturated ground continued to promote mud and rock slides across eastern Kentucky, complete with power outages from fallen debris and trees. Pikeville experienced a massive rockslide early on February 22, resulting in several cars and a building being damaged. Other impacts included countless roads being inundated with standing water, pavement and water pipes breaking, water threatening homes, roads and bridges being washed away, and rising lake levels.||Of the 33 counties across eastern Kentucky, at least 27 were forced to declare State of Emergencies due to rainfall during and before this event. This included Knott County due to over $150,000 in county road damages. Beattyville, Clay City, Harlan, Hazard, Jenkins, Monticello, Paintsville, Pikeville, Salyersville, Stanton, West Liberty, and Whitesburg also declared State of Emergencies. Additionally, the Kentucky Governor declared a State of Emergency across the entire Commonwealth.||The persistent rainfall of the winter season and the amount of rainfall from this event led to Lake Cumberland reporting their highest lake level of 756.52 feet on February 26th, breaking the old record of 751.69 feet dating back to May 1984. As a result, Wolf Creek Dam had to release at a record discharge level of 59,880 cubic feet per second. Cave Run Lake also rose to within 6 feet of their record pool elevation. Buckhorn Lake came within 10 feet of their all-time record, partially resulting in Kentucky Highway 257 near Confluence having flood waters cover the highway isolating 40 to 50 families for three weeks following the end of the flooding rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Department of Highways reported a major break in pavement, made worse by heavy rain on February 23rd, on Kentucky Highway 705 near Grassy Creek. This led to an extended closure of the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another very moist storm system similar to the system on the 7th brought widespread heavy rainfall. Rainfall amounts from 2 to 4 inches were common during a 48-hour period. Isolated higher amounts near 5 inches occurred near the Tennessee border from Fulton to Murray. This heavy rain fell on ground that was saturated from previous heavy rain events earlier in the month, causing rapid runoff. A weak trough passed south across the area and then moved back north as a warm front as an area of low pressure developed in the southern Plains and moved east along the front. Widespread showers brought heavy downpours before a cold front moved east on the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kentucky 1379 was closed between the 0 and 3 mile markers due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major river flooding developed during the month. After a wet January, the active weather pattern continued into February. Frequent moderate to heavy rain events continued pushing not only the monthly and seasonal precipitation totals higher but also the rivers. The winter of 2018-19 ended up being the fourth wettest winter on record at Paducah, where records go back to 1937. February was the second wettest February on record at Paducah. The monthly rainfall total at Paducah was 10.42 inches. Persistent south to southwest winds aloft brought a steady supply of moisture from the Pacific or the Gulf of Mexico into the region. Fronts that lifted north across the region brought southerly winds and moisture into the lower levels. When the two coincided, areas of very heavy rain occurred. Record rainfall in the Cumberland and Tennessee Valleys sent a large volume of water into Kentucky and Barkley Lakes. Both lakes crested around 367 feet, 8 feet above their summer pool stage. For many locations on the lower Ohio River, it was the highest stage since the historic 2011 flood. The flood crest at Paducah was tied with the 1950 flood for the second highest since the Flood of 1937. The 2011 flood remains the worst flood since the Flood of 1937 at Paducah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low-lying portions of the Calvert City area were flooded by the Tennessee River. Huge releases from Kentucky Dam contributed to the high levels on the river. Major flooding on the Ohio River caused water to back up into the Tennessee River. As a result, some of the industrial area of Calvert City was flooded. Primarily parking lots and access roads were flooded, but some storage sheds and buildings were sandbagged. Several  residences were sandbagged, with water in yards and outbuildings. A few streets were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 19, 2019, a broad upper trough dipped south to the Gulf of Mexico and carried abundant amounts of moisture towards the Ohio Valley. Once the moisture was transport was underway, isentropic lift caused 1.5 to 3 of rainfall along the Mississippi and Ohio River Valleys. The higher amounts went as far north as south central Indiana.||On the 20th, an upper trough axis and cold front pushed through southern Indiana and central Kentucky. The heaviest rain fell during the morning and into the afternoon hours before tapering off from west to east late on the 20th.||Moving into the 22nd, the upper flow amplified once again with a deep southwest flow aloft.  Isentropic lift was underway resulting in widespread light rain pushing northward from Tennessee into Kentucky during the day.  By that night, the ridge increased slightly across the region with a surface warm front pushing northward.  More moderate to heavy rainfall fell during the night which caused localized flooding. ||On the evening of the 23rd, surface low pressure in the vicinity of the Kansas City, MO area with an arcing cold front pushed towards the Mississippi River.  This cold front pushed through the region during the night and brought more moderate to heavy |rain along with some thunderstorms. ||All this rain and the saturated ground caused many flooding problems around central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Logan County Dispatch reported that several roads across the city and county were closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted northward into Kentucky on the evening of the 23rd, promoting widespread showers and a few isolated thunderstorms due to the close proximity of an approaching cold front. Almost two inches of rain fell between the evening of the 23rd and the morning of the 24th.||Behind the cold front, very gusty winds developed with most areas seeing gusts of 40 to 50 knots. Combined with the soggy ground, these winds led to power outages due to downed trees and power lines. Nearly a thousand power customers lost power, and it took a couple days for all service to be restored.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Sandy River flooded near Grayson, flooding farm land and low spots on roadways along the river. ||The river gage showed the river rose above its 21 foot flood stage the morning of the 24th. It crested at nearly 21.4 feet just before noon and returned to its banks by mid afternoon.

Periods of rain worked back into eastern Kentucky early on February 20 as a warm frontal boundary lifted north across the Commonwealth. A cold front brought additional rainfall through the afternoon and evening, before drier conditions worked into eastern Kentucky during the morning of February 21. ||Rain quickly spread back north that evening into the 22nd with southeastern Kentucky continuing to see prolonged light rain into the afternoon of the 23rd. A cold front brought additional rain to much of eastern Kentucky late on the 23rd. ||A spike in moisture ahead of this front resulted in additional moderate to heavy rain and high rainfall rates across portions of eastern Kentucky late on the 23rd. The convection ahead of the cold front that evening resulted in the majority of the flooding and flash flooding issues across eastern Kentucky. Showers and thunderstorms capable of heavy rainfall trained over parts of Wayne, Pulaski, Rockcastle, and other counties along the I-75 corridor, resulting in deadly flash flooding and river flooding along the Cumberland River. Later in the evening, the bulk of the rainfall shifted north to counties along the Mountain Parkway. Flash flooding and river flooding occurred in Powell, Wolfe, Breathitt, Magoffin, and Johnson Counties. Lighter rainfall continued into the night as flooding issues remained widespread into the 24th.||Rainfall amounts from this event ranged from near 2 inches in portions of the Bluegrass region and northeast Kentucky, to greater than 4 inches across locations in southern and southeast Kentucky. ||This additional water on top of saturated ground continued to promote mud and rock slides across eastern Kentucky, complete with power outages from fallen debris and trees. Pikeville experienced a massive rockslide early on February 22, resulting in several cars and a building being damaged. Other impacts included countless roads being inundated with standing water, pavement and water pipes breaking, water threatening homes, roads and bridges being washed away, and rising lake levels.||Of the 33 counties across eastern Kentucky, at least 27 were forced to declare State of Emergencies due to rainfall during and before this event. This included Knott County due to over $150,000 in county road damages. Beattyville, Clay City, Harlan, Hazard, Jenkins, Monticello, Paintsville, Pikeville, Salyersville, Stanton, West Liberty, and Whitesburg also declared State of Emergencies. Additionally, the Kentucky Governor declared a State of Emergency across the entire Commonwealth.||The persistent rainfall of the winter season and the amount of rainfall from this event led to Lake Cumberland reporting their highest lake level of 756.52 feet on February 26th, breaking the old record of 751.69 feet dating back to May 1984. As a result, Wolf Creek Dam had to release at a record discharge level of 59,880 cubic feet per second. Cave Run Lake also rose to within 6 feet of their record pool elevation. Buckhorn Lake came within 10 feet of their all-time record, partially resulting in Kentucky Highway 257 near Confluence having flood waters cover the highway isolating 40 to 50 families for three weeks following the end of the flooding rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local media outlet reported mud and rock slides from near the Estill County line on Kentucky Highway 52 to Blaines Branch Road in Beattyville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another very moist storm system similar to the system on the 7th brought widespread heavy rainfall. Rainfall amounts from 2 to 4 inches were common during a 48-hour period. Isolated higher amounts near 5 inches occurred near the Tennessee border from Fulton to Murray. This heavy rain fell on ground that was saturated from previous heavy rain events earlier in the month, causing rapid runoff. A weak trough passed south across the area and then moved back north as a warm front as an area of low pressure developed in the southern Plains and moved east along the front. Widespread showers brought heavy downpours before a cold front moved east on the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were closed throughout the county due to flooding. Most of the roads were city streets in Murray or lesser travelled county roads. A trained spotter eight miles northeast of Murray measured 4.47 inches in 48 hours. A Cocorahs observer at Murray measured 4.82 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved slowly northward from the Gulf Coast states on the night of the 19th, reaching the lower Ohio Valley on the morning of the 20th. This warm front brought the third widespread heavy rainfall event so far in February. Rainfall totals over a 24-hour period were from 2 to 3 inches in much of western Kentucky, with locally up to 3.5 inches west of Kentucky Lake. This rainfall fell on moist ground, causing many creeks and smaller rivers to flood. The flooding was exacerbated by very high levels on the Ohio and Mississippi Rivers, which caused tributary rivers and creeks to back up into western Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A street intersection on the south side of Henderson was closed by flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tradewater River flooded large areas of fields and woodlands, along with some state roads including Highway 120.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major river flooding developed during the month. After a wet January, the active weather pattern continued into February. Frequent moderate to heavy rain events continued pushing not only the monthly and seasonal precipitation totals higher but also the rivers. The winter of 2018-19 ended up being the fourth wettest winter on record at Paducah, where records go back to 1937. February was the second wettest February on record at Paducah. The monthly rainfall total at Paducah was 10.42 inches. Persistent south to southwest winds aloft brought a steady supply of moisture from the Pacific or the Gulf of Mexico into the region. Fronts that lifted north across the region brought southerly winds and moisture into the lower levels. When the two coincided, areas of very heavy rain occurred. Record rainfall in the Cumberland and Tennessee Valleys sent a large volume of water into Kentucky and Barkley Lakes. Both lakes crested around 367 feet, 8 feet above their summer pool stage. For many locations on the lower Ohio River, it was the highest stage since the historic 2011 flood. The flood crest at Paducah was tied with the 1950 flood for the second highest since the Flood of 1937. The 2011 flood remains the worst flood since the Flood of 1937 at Paducah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The level of Kentucky Lake was raised to 367 feet, about 8 feet above the normal summer pool stage. Low-lying parks, campgrounds, and marina parking lots near the lake were flooded. Some lake access roads were underwater. The lake level was still several feet below the record set during the spring of 2011 flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 19, 2019, a broad upper trough dipped south to the Gulf of Mexico and carried abundant amounts of moisture towards the Ohio Valley. Once the moisture was transport was underway, isentropic lift caused 1.5 to 3 of rainfall along the Mississippi and Ohio River Valleys. The higher amounts went as far north as south central Indiana.||On the 20th, an upper trough axis and cold front pushed through southern Indiana and central Kentucky. The heaviest rain fell during the morning and into the afternoon hours before tapering off from west to east late on the 20th.||Moving into the 22nd, the upper flow amplified once again with a deep southwest flow aloft.  Isentropic lift was underway resulting in widespread light rain pushing northward from Tennessee into Kentucky during the day.  By that night, the ridge increased slightly across the region with a surface warm front pushing northward.  More moderate to heavy rainfall fell during the night which caused localized flooding. ||On the evening of the 23rd, surface low pressure in the vicinity of the Kansas City, MO area with an arcing cold front pushed towards the Mississippi River.  This cold front pushed through the region during the night and brought more moderate to heavy |rain along with some thunderstorms. ||All this rain and the saturated ground caused many flooding problems around central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A bridge on Blackgold-Grassland Road had to be closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system strengthened as it moved northeast into the Great Lakes region. A large area of showers and thunderstorms associated with a warm front moved across the region during the afternoon of the 23rd, producing heavy rain and some flash flooding. The hardest-hit areas were in the Kentucky counties bordering Tennessee, including the city of Murray. From two to four inches of rain fell in the southern border counties of western Kentucky, mostly within a four-hour period. Behind a trailing cold front, west winds gusted from 40 to 50 mph late on the night of the 23rd into the early morning of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 58 was flooded in Clinton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major river flooding developed during the month. After a wet January, the active weather pattern continued into February. Frequent moderate to heavy rain events continued pushing not only the monthly and seasonal precipitation totals higher but also the rivers. The winter of 2018-19 ended up being the fourth wettest winter on record at Paducah, where records go back to 1937. February was the second wettest February on record at Paducah. The monthly rainfall total at Paducah was 10.42 inches. Persistent south to southwest winds aloft brought a steady supply of moisture from the Pacific or the Gulf of Mexico into the region. Fronts that lifted north across the region brought southerly winds and moisture into the lower levels. When the two coincided, areas of very heavy rain occurred. Record rainfall in the Cumberland and Tennessee Valleys sent a large volume of water into Kentucky and Barkley Lakes. Both lakes crested around 367 feet, 8 feet above their summer pool stage. For many locations on the lower Ohio River, it was the highest stage since the historic 2011 flood. The flood crest at Paducah was tied with the 1950 flood for the second highest since the Flood of 1937. The 2011 flood remains the worst flood since the Flood of 1937 at Paducah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The level of Kentucky Lake was raised to 367 feet, about 8 feet above the normal summer pool stage. Low-lying parks, campgrounds, and marina parking lots near the lake were flooded. Some lake access roads were underwater. The lake level was still several feet below the record set during the spring of 2011 flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 19, 2019, a broad upper trough dipped south to the Gulf of Mexico and carried abundant amounts of moisture towards the Ohio Valley. Once the moisture was transport was underway, isentropic lift caused 1.5 to 3 of rainfall along the Mississippi and Ohio River Valleys. The higher amounts went as far north as south central Indiana.||On the 20th, an upper trough axis and cold front pushed through southern Indiana and central Kentucky. The heaviest rain fell during the morning and into the afternoon hours before tapering off from west to east late on the 20th.||Moving into the 22nd, the upper flow amplified once again with a deep southwest flow aloft.  Isentropic lift was underway resulting in widespread light rain pushing northward from Tennessee into Kentucky during the day.  By that night, the ridge increased slightly across the region with a surface warm front pushing northward.  More moderate to heavy rainfall fell during the night which caused localized flooding. ||On the evening of the 23rd, surface low pressure in the vicinity of the Kansas City, MO area with an arcing cold front pushed towards the Mississippi River.  This cold front pushed through the region during the night and brought more moderate to heavy |rain along with some thunderstorms. ||All this rain and the saturated ground caused many flooding problems around central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads were closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving storms developed during the late afternoon and early��evening of June 6th. The slow movement produced locally heavy rainfall which produced some flash flooding in a few communities of eastern Kentucky, including Rockcastle and Powell Counties.||In Rockcastle County, at least 6 businesses, a church, and city hall were inundated with water in Brodhead. Several local, county, and state highways were closed during the peak of the flooding. A trailer park had water flowing up to about 10 trailers, while two storage buildings were washed off of their foundation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mini supercell thunderstorms developed across eastern Kentucky this afternoon as an upper level system swung through the Ohio Valley. Several of these storms produced damaging winds, including after they merged into line segments. Downed trees resulted in damage to a few homes and vehicles as well as numerous power outages, but fortunately no injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quick moving shower, on top of recent rainfall, led to Flat Lick Creek overflowing its banks onto Barnesburg and Mark Welburn Roads as observed by a trained spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms tracked across Kentucky this afternoon, reaching eastern Kentucky and the Interstate 75 corridor during the early evening. This line produced numerous reports of wind damage, including the destruction of a mobile home in East Bernstadt due to a tree falling onto it. In Monticello, a tree was struck by lightning, causing it to fall onto a house while also downing a powerline. This line of storms began to weaken as the sun set during the mid evening hours, losing much of its intensity by the time it reached U.S. Highway 23.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Department of Highways observed a tree down on Jake Ridge Road near Ary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe weather outbreak occurred during the afternoon and early evening hours. An organized line of severe thunderstorms formed over the central Plains states during the night of the 20th. This bowing line of storms progressed southeast across the St. Louis area during the midday hours, then down across the lower Ohio Valley and southeast Missouri during the afternoon. Widespread wind damage was reported in parts of western Kentucky, along with several weak to moderately strong tornadoes. Very large hail was reported in a few locations. The atmosphere ahead of the storms became strongly unstable, with surface-based capes exceeding 4,000. Given the strong buoyancy, wind profiles were sufficiently strong for widespread severe weather. Deep-layer (0-6 km) wind shear values were no higher than about 30 knots, and 0-1 km shear was about 20 knots. Even though the region was under a broad 500 mb ridge, a moderately strong low-level feed of very moist and unstable air fueled the intense storms as they propagated southeastward.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived complex of thunderstorms, originating across the central Plains and Midwest, moved through Kentucky late this afternoon and evening. A tight instability gradient kept the strongest portions of this line of storms confined to the Lake Cumberland region and southeast parts of Kentucky locally. ||The most significant damage occurred across portions of Pulaski and Wayne Counties, including over 4,000 power outages due to downed trees and powerlines. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms tracked across Kentucky this afternoon, reaching eastern Kentucky and the Interstate 75 corridor during the early evening. This line produced numerous reports of wind damage, including the destruction of a mobile home in East Bernstadt due to a tree falling onto it. In Monticello, a tree was struck by lightning, causing it to fall onto a house while also downing a powerline. This line of storms began to weaken as the sun set during the mid evening hours, losing much of its intensity by the time it reached U.S. Highway 23.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported trees down north of London, at the intersection of Dollie Miller Road and Kentucky Highway 1376, as well as on McWhorter Road. A tree blew onto a mobile home in East Bernstadt, destroying it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters and short lines of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours. Several of these were accompanied by damaging winds, measured up to 64 mph at the Murray, KY airport. The storms developed south of a surface low pressure center that gradually deepened as it tracked east along the Interstate 70 corridor from Missouri to Indiana. Thunderstorm development was enhanced by large-scale ascent pivoting from the Ozark Plateau into the lower Ohio Valley, ahead of a low amplitude mid-level trough. Daytime heating helped to increase instability, with mixed layer capes approaching 2000 J/kg. Vertical wind shear increased as 40 to 50-knot westerly winds gradually propagated  eastward from the south central Plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power line was down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of rain on November 30th led to flooding in portions of Powell and Estill Counties on December 1 and 2 as rainfall totals of nearly 3.5 inches were observed. Road closures were the primary impacts as flood waters slowly receded along the Kentucky and Red Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter observed water from the Red River spilling over onto Kentucky Highway 2026 northwest of Stanton, forcing the closure of the highway.

A fireworks tent and utility pole at the Cumberland Crossing Shopping Center in Monticello were blown down by severe wind gusts, while a few tin roofs were also blown down in and north of Monticello. This led to the declaration of a State of Emergency to assist with cleanup efforts. South of Somerset, a large oak tree was blown down onto a house, causing extensive damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county Emergency Manager and public reported numerous trees down in and north of Monticello along Kentucky Highway 90. These downed trees resulted in powerlines being knocked down, leading to over 4,000 power outages across the county. A few tin roofs were also blown off of buildings, further complicating road accessibility for cleanup crews. A State of Emergency was declared in order to speed up cleanup efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms blew down trees and utility lines across Laurel and Leslie Counties, while also causing flash flooding across portions of Laurel County on June 30, 2019. Downed utility lines near Wendover in Leslie County resulted in a three car pileup on U.S. Highway 421. The city of London experienced flash flooding for a short period of time late in the evening of June 30th into the early hours of July 1st. Several roads had 6 to 12 inches of water flowing across them and some homes in the area had the same amount of water flowing through their yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported utility lines were blown down and onto U.S. Highway 421 near Wendover, resulting in a 3 car pileup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters and short lines of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours. Several of these were accompanied by damaging winds, measured up to 64 mph at the Murray, KY airport. The storms developed south of a surface low pressure center that gradually deepened as it tracked east along the Interstate 70 corridor from Missouri to Indiana. Thunderstorm development was enhanced by large-scale ascent pivoting from the Ozark Plateau into the lower Ohio Valley, ahead of a low amplitude mid-level trough. Daytime heating helped to increase instability, with mixed layer capes approaching 2000 J/kg. Vertical wind shear increased as 40 to 50-knot westerly winds gradually propagated  eastward from the south central Plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 64 mph at the Murray-Calloway County Airport. Two trees and power lines were down on Highway 80 near the airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms blew down trees and utility lines across Laurel and Leslie Counties, while also causing flash flooding across portions of Laurel County on June 30, 2019. Downed utility lines near Wendover in Leslie County resulted in a three car pileup on U.S. Highway 421. The city of London experienced flash flooding for a short period of time late in the evening of June 30th into the early hours of July 1st. Several roads had 6 to 12 inches of water flowing across them and some homes in the area had the same amount of water flowing through their yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local Emergency Management reported multiple trees were blown down from Lily to Fariston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of showers, producing brief heavy rain, occurred early on June 10th. This on top of previous heavy rain over the past several days led to isolated flash flooding near Herd. Additionally, a thunderstorm near Hazel Green produced a cloud to ground lightning strike which struck a field and resulted in the death of a calf.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county Emergency Manager reported one foot of water flowing over Kentucky Highway 1431 southeast of Herd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Loosely organized clusters of thunderstorms produced isolated flash flooding, along with isolated damaging winds and hail. A mesoscale vorticity center from earlier thunderstorms moved eastward across southern Missouri during the afternoon. Very warm and moist air in advance of this vorticity center contributed to strong instability, with mixed-layer capes around 3000. Around 25 to 40 knots of westerly flow in the mid levels of the atmosphere was sufficient for loosely organized thunderstorm clusters, a few of which were severe.

Slow-moving thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused isolated minor flooding problems. The storms developed in response to daytime heating of a very moist air mass. A low pressure system drifted slowly eastward into the lower Mississippi Valley, tapping into a fetch of tropical moisture from the Gulf of Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was reported. Standing water was over some roads due to storm drains not keeping up with very heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving storms developed during the late afternoon and early��evening of June 6th. The slow movement produced locally heavy rainfall which produced some flash flooding in a few communities of eastern Kentucky, including Rockcastle and Powell Counties.||In Rockcastle County, at least 6 businesses, a church, and city hall were inundated with water in Brodhead. Several local, county, and state highways were closed during the peak of the flooding. Two storage buildings were washed off of their foundations, while about 10 mobile homes were damaged as nearly 4 inches of rain fell in a 2 1/2 hour window during the late afternoon into the early evening of the 6th.||In Powell County, flash flooding occurred in and around the Natural Bridge State Park area, northward to the Slade exit off the Mountain Parkway. Multiple water rescues were performed as many homes became surrounded by water. A water rescue took place at the 5900 block of High Rock Road, where 2 people were trapped in their car surrounded by flood waters. Multiple locations along Highway 11 had flowing water across the road. Several cars were at least partially submerged. Little Abner's Motel near the park��was also flooded out.||Light to moderate rain continued through June 7, producing additional minor issues. These included mudslides and road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kentucky Transportation Cabinet reported a mudslide on Kentucky Highway 1087 south of Clay City, due to prior heavy rainfall.

The line moved very slowly in some areas, resulting in isolated flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe weather outbreak occurred during the afternoon and early evening hours. Widespread wind damage was reported in parts of western Kentucky, along with several weak to moderately strong tornadoes. Very large hail was reported in a few locations. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms sank southward across northwest Kentucky, producing isolated wind damage. The thunderstorm complex moved southward on the east side of a mid-level high centered over Missouri. A warm, moist, and unstable air mass provided a favorable environment for the storms to maintain their intensity into the lower Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were scattered reports of downed trees and power lines across the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued on the lowest reaches of the Ohio River due to backwater from the swollen Mississippi River. The region remained stuck in a long-term wet pattern that continued through the spring and into early summer. Most locations were 1 to 2 inches above normal for the month of June. Some locations saw as much as 3 to 5 inches more rainfall than normal in June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ohio River remained above flood stage since February 8 and through the month of June. This was the most prolonged flood on the Ohio River since records began at Cairo, Illinois in the late 1800's. The highest recorded stage in the month of June (2019) at Cairo was 51.54 feet, which corresponds to the moderate flood category. At this stage, the flooding has a significant impact on farming operations in the flood plain, causing some farmers to lose crops. However, farmers never were able to plant crops because this flood began in the winter. Riverside parks and recreational facilities remained underwater. Some side roads near the river were flooded. Due to the extended duration of the flooding, levees along the river were very stressed from saturation of the soil. Seepage was a concern. Considerable flooding of low-lying woods, fields, and a wildlife management area persisted through the month. Moderate flooding continued into the month of July.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down near Lovelaceville in Ballard County, but the vast majority of the track was in McCracken County. Peak winds in Ballard County were estimated near 100 mph. This was the third and longest of three tornadoes that occurred along a path from western Carlisle County to McCracken County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Loosely organized clusters of thunderstorms produced isolated flash flooding, along with isolated damaging winds and hail. A mesoscale vorticity center from earlier thunderstorms moved eastward across southern Missouri during the afternoon. Very warm and moist air in advance of this vorticity center contributed to strong instability, with mixed-layer capes around 3000. Around 25 to 40 knots of westerly flow in the mid levels of the atmosphere was sufficient for loosely organized thunderstorm clusters, a few of which were severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lots of large limbs were down. Several trees were snapped. There was minor shingle damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began east of the Woodlands Trace and Silver Trail Road junction and tracked southeast along Silver Trail Road before crossing into Trigg County. Hundreds of trees, some very large, were snapped or uprooted. The path of this tornado was in densely wooded areas of the Land Between The Lakes National Recreation Area. Peak winds were estimated near 100 mph. Most of the path was in Lyon County, however a popular tourist spot was struck in the Trigg County portion.
